title.zh: 卷四十四 淮南衡山濟北王傳
title.en: Volume 44: The kings of Huainan, Hengshan and Jibei
title.raw: 卷四十四 淮南衡山濟北王傳 Volume 44: The kings of Huainan, Hengshan and Jibei

sentences.zh: 淮南厲王長，高帝少子也，其母故趙王張敖美人。

translations.literal: The Prince Li of Huainan, Liu Chang, was a younger son of the Supreme Emperor; his mother had been a beauty of King Zhang Ao of Zhao.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Chang, posthumously known as the Prince Li of Huainan, was one of the Gaozu Emperor's younger sons. His mother had been a concubine of Zhang Ao, the king of Zhao.

sentences.zh: 高帝八年，從東垣過趙，趙王獻美人，厲王母也，幸，有身。

translations.literal: In the eighth year of the Supreme Emperor, passing through Zhao from Dongyuan, the King of Zhao presented a beauty—she was the mother of Prince Li—who received favor and became pregnant.
translations.idiomatic: In 199 BCE, while returning from Dongyuan through Zhao, the king of Zhao offered him a woman who became Prince Li's mother. She entered the emperor's favor and conceived.

sentences.zh: 趙王不敢內宮，為築外宮捨之。

translations.literal: The King of Zhao did not dare bring her into the inner palace; he built an outer palace and lodged her there.
translations.idiomatic: The king of Zhao dared not house her in his inner quarters, so he had a separate residence built outside the palace and kept her there.

sentences.zh: 及貫高等謀反事覺，並逮治王，盡捕王母兄弟美人，系之河內。

translations.literal: When the plotting to rebel by Guan Gao and others was discovered, together they seized and prosecuted the king, capturing all the king's mother, brothers, and beauties, and bound them in Henei.
translations.idiomatic: When Guan Gao's plot to assassinate the emperor came to light, the king of Zhao was arrested along with everyone connected to him—his mother, his brothers, his concubines—and they were all held in custody in Henei.

sentences.zh: 厲王母亦系，告吏曰：「日得幸上，有子。」

translations.literal: The mother of Prince Li was also bound; she told the clerks, saying, "I once received favor from the Supreme One; I have a son."
translations.idiomatic: Prince Li's mother was among the prisoners. She told the officials, "The emperor favored me once—I am carrying his child."

sentences.zh: 吏以聞，上方怒趙，未及理厲王母。

translations.literal: The clerks reported it; the Supreme One was then angry at Zhao and had not yet reached to dealing with the mother of Prince Li.
translations.idiomatic: They passed the word upward, but Gaozu was still furious with Zhao and had no attention to spare for her case.

sentences.zh: 厲王母弟趙兼因辟陽侯言呂後，呂後妒，不肯白，辟陽侯不強爭。

translations.literal: The younger brother of the mother of Prince Li, Zhao Jian, through the Marquis of Piyang spoke to Empress Lü; Empress Lü was jealous and was unwilling to speak plainly; the Marquis of Piyang did not forcefully contend.
translations.idiomatic: Her brother Zhao Jian appealed through the Marquis of Piyang to Empress Lü, but Lü was jealous and refused to put in a word for her. The marquis did not press the matter.

sentences.zh: 厲王母已生厲生，恚，即自殺。

translations.literal: The mother of Prince Li had already given birth to him; resentful, she thereupon killed herself.
translations.idiomatic: Once she had delivered the child who would later be enfeoffed as Prince Li of Huainan, she killed herself in bitterness and despair.

sentences.zh: 吏奉厲王詣上，上悔，令呂後母之，而葬其母真定。

translations.literal: The clerks presented Prince Li to the Supreme One; the Supreme One regretted it, ordered Empress Lü to mother him, and buried his mother at Zhending.
translations.idiomatic: Officials brought the infant to the emperor, who was stricken with remorse. He had Empress Lü raise the child as her own and had the mother buried in Zhending.

sentences.zh: 真定，厲王母家縣也。

translations.literal: Zhending was the home county of the mother of Prince Li.
translations.idiomatic: Zhending was her family's home county.

sentences.zh: 十一年，淮南王布反，上自將擊滅布，即立子長為淮南子。

translations.literal: In the eleventh year, the King of Huainan, Bu, rebelled; the Supreme One personally led troops, struck and destroyed Bu, and thereupon established his son Chang as Prince of Huainan.
translations.idiomatic: In 196 BCE, when Ying Bu, king of Huainan, rose in revolt, Gaozu led the army in person, crushed him, and enfeoffed his son Liu Chang as the new king of Huainan.

sentences.zh: 王早失母，常附呂後，孝惠、呂後時以故得幸無患，然常心怨辟陽侯，不敢發。

translations.literal: The king had early lost his mother, constantly attached himself to Empress Lü, and in the time of Emperor Xiaohui and Empress Lü for this reason received favor without calamity; yet constantly in his heart he resented the Marquis of Piyang, not daring to give vent.
translations.idiomatic: Orphaned young, he had grown up under Empress Lü's wing and was spared during the reigns of Emperor Hui and the Lü empresses, but he never forgave the Marquis of Piyang—only he dared not show it.

sentences.zh: 及孝文初即位，自以為最親，驕蹇，數不奉法。

translations.literal: When Emperor Xiaowen had first ascended the throne, he considered himself the closest kin, was arrogant and refractory, and repeatedly did not observe the laws.
translations.idiomatic: Once Emperor Wen took the throne, Liu Chang assumed that as the emperor's only full brother he could do as he pleased. He grew arrogant, flouted the law again and again, and treated the court with contempt.

sentences.zh: 上寬赦之。

translations.literal: The Supreme One broadly pardoned him.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor repeatedly overlooked his offenses.

sentences.zh: 三年，入朝，甚橫。

translations.literal: In the third year, he entered court audience, very overbearing.
translations.idiomatic: In the third year of Wen's reign he came to the capital and behaved with shocking arrogance.

sentences.zh: 從上入苑獵，與上同輦，常謂上「大兄」。

translations.literal: Following the Supreme One into the park to hunt, riding in the same carriage with the Supreme One, he constantly called the Supreme One "great elder brother."
translations.idiomatic: He rode beside the emperor in the imperial carriage when they hunted in the park and habitually addressed him as "big brother" instead of as his sovereign.

sentences.zh: 厲王有材力，力扛鼎，乃往請辟陽侯。

translations.literal: Prince Li had talent and strength; his strength could shoulder a tripod; he thereupon went to request an audience with the Marquis of Piyang.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Chang was powerfully built—strong enough to lift a bronze cauldron—and he went to call on the Marquis of Piyang.

sentences.zh: 辟陽侯出見之，即自袖金椎椎之，命從者刑之。

translations.literal: The Marquis of Piyang came out to see him; he immediately from his sleeve took a metal mallet and mallet-struck him, ordering followers to execute him.
translations.idiomatic: When the marquis came out to greet him, Liu Chang produced a metal hammer from his sleeve, struck him down, and had his attendants finish the killing.

sentences.zh: 馳詣闕下，肉袒而謝曰：「臣母不當坐趙時事，辟陽侯力能得之呂後，不爭，罪一也。

translations.literal: He galloped to below the palace gate, bared his upper body, and apologized, saying, "My mother ought not to have been implicated in the affairs of Zhao; the Marquis of Piyang had strength enough to obtain her release from Empress Lü, yet did not contend—this is his first crime."
translations.idiomatic: He raced to the palace gate, stripped to the waist in token of submission, and said, "My mother should never have been punished for the Zhao affair. The Marquis of Piyang could have persuaded Empress Lü to spare her, but he would not speak up—that is his first offense against me."

sentences.zh: 趙王如意子母無罪，呂後殺之，辟陽侯不爭，罪二也。

translations.literal: The King of Zhao Ruyi, mother and son, were without guilt; Empress Lü killed them; the Marquis of Piyang did not contend—this is his second crime.
translations.idiomatic: Prince Ruyi of Zhao and his mother were innocent, yet Empress Lü murdered them while the marquis stood silent—that is his second offense.

sentences.zh: 呂後王諸呂，欲以危劉氏，辟陽侯不爭，罪三也。

translations.literal: Empress Lü ennobled the Lüs as kings, intending thereby to endanger the house of Liu; the Marquis of Piyang did not contend—this is his third crime.
translations.idiomatic: When Empress Lü made her kinsmen kings and set out to destroy the Liu house, he raised no objection—that is his third offense.

sentences.zh: 臣謹為天下誅賊，報母之仇，伏闕下請罪。」

translations.literal: Your servant respectfully for the realm executed the villain, avenging his mother's wrong; he prostrates below the gate and requests punishment."
translations.idiomatic: I have rid the empire of a traitor and avenged my mother. I throw myself on your mercy beneath the gate."

sentences.zh: 文帝傷其志，為親故不治，赦之。

translations.literal: Emperor Xiaowen was moved by his intent; because of kinship he did not prosecute and pardoned him.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wen pitied the passion behind the act and, unwilling to punish his own brother, let him go.

sentences.zh: 當是時，自薄太后及太子諸大臣皆憚厲王，厲王以此歸國益恣，不用漢法，出入警蹕，稱制，自作法令，數上書不遜順。

translations.literal: At this time, from Empress Dowager Bo down to the heir apparent and the great ministers, all feared Prince Li; Prince Li for this reason returning to his state became more wanton, did not use Han law, in going out and in had clearing of the way and prohibition of passage, assumed imperial style, himself made laws and orders, and repeatedly submitted memorials not submissive and compliant.
translations.idiomatic: From Empress Dowager Bo to the crown prince and the senior ministers, everyone walked in fear of him. Emboldened, he returned to Huainan more lawless than ever: he ignored Han statutes, cleared the roads as an emperor would, issued edicts in his own name, drafted his own laws, and sent memorial after memorial in a tone that brooked no obedience.

sentences.zh: 文帝重自切責之。

translations.literal: Emperor Xiaowen repeatedly himself sternly reproached him.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor admonished him sternly, again and again.

sentences.zh: 時帝舅薄昭為將軍，尊重，上令昭予厲王書諫數之，曰：

translations.literal: At that time the emperor's maternal uncle Bo Zhao was a general, honored and weighty; the Supreme One ordered Zhao to give Prince Li a letter remonstrating and enumerating his faults, saying:
translations.idiomatic: The emperor turned to his uncle Bo Zhao, a respected general, and had him draft a letter of rebuke listing Liu Chang's transgressions. It read:

sentences.zh: 竊聞大王剛直而勇，慈惠而厚，貞信多斷，是天以聖人之資奉大王也甚盛，不可不察。

translations.literal: I have heard in private that the Great King is firm and straight yet brave, kind and gracious yet generous, chaste and trustworthy and much decisive; this is Heaven with the endowment of a sage serving the Great King in very fullness—it cannot but be examined.
translations.idiomatic: I have heard that Your Highness combines courage with integrity, generosity with kindness, and sound judgment with steadfast honor—gifts Heaven has lavished on you as on a sage. You owe it to yourself to reflect on that.

sentences.zh: 今大王所行，不稱天資。

translations.literal: Now what the Great King practices does not match Heaven's endowment.
translations.idiomatic: What you have been doing of late is unworthy of those gifts.

sentences.zh: 皇帝初即位，易侯邑在淮南者，大王不肯。

translations.literal: When the Emperor had first ascended the throne, he shifted the fiefs of marquises located in Huainan; the Great King was unwilling.
translations.idiomatic: When the emperor first came to the throne, he proposed moving certain marquises' fiefs out of Huainan, and you refused.

sentences.zh: 皇帝卒易之，使大王得三縣之實，甚厚。

translations.literal: The Emperor finally shifted them, causing the Great King to obtain the substance of three counties—very generous.
translations.idiomatic: In the end he went through with the transfer and let you keep the revenue of three counties instead—a remarkably generous settlement.

sentences.zh: 大王以未嘗與皇帝相見，求入朝見，未畢昆弟之歡，而殺列侯以自為名。

translations.literal: The Great King, because he had never met the Emperor, sought to enter court and have audience; before the joy of brotherhood was completed, he killed a full marquis to make a name for himself.
translations.idiomatic: You asked leave to visit the capital because you had never met your imperial brother face to face, yet before you could enjoy a true reunion you murdered a marquis of the first rank to win notoriety.

sentences.zh: 皇帝不使吏與其間，赦大王，甚厚。

translations.literal: The Emperor did not send clerks to involve themselves in the matter and pardoned the Great King—very generous.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor declined to set the law on you and granted a full pardon—again, extraordinary indulgence.

sentences.zh: 漢法，二千石缺，輒言漢補，大王逐漢所置，而請自置相、二千石。

translations.literal: By Han law, when a two-thousand-dan post is vacant, one always reports to Han to fill it; the Great King drove out those Han had appointed and requested to appoint himself chancellor and two-thousand-dan officials.
translations.idiomatic: Statute requires that vacancies among the two-thousand-dan officials be filled from the center; you expelled the men the court had posted and demanded the right to name your own chancellor and senior officers.

sentences.zh: 皇帝骫天下正法而許大王，甚厚。

translations.literal: The Emperor bent the correct law of all under Heaven and granted the Great King—very generous.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor strained the law of the empire to grant even that—a depth of favor you can hardly repay.

sentences.zh: 大王欲屬國為布衣，守塚真定。

translations.literal: The Great King wished to hand over the state and become a commoner, guarding the tomb at Zhending.
translations.idiomatic: You asked to surrender your kingdom, live as a commoner, and tend your mother's grave in Zhending.

sentences.zh: 皇帝不許，使大王毋失南面之尊，甚厚。

translations.literal: The Emperor did not permit it, causing the Great King not to lose the honor of facing south—very generous.
translations.idiomatic: He would not hear of it and insisted that you keep the dignity of a ruling prince—yet another mark of his kindness.

sentences.zh: 大王宜日夜奉法度，修貢職，以稱皇帝之厚德，今乃輕言恣行，以負謗於天下，甚非計也。

translations.literal: The Great King ought day and night to uphold the laws and regulations, cultivate tribute duties, thereby to match the Emperor's thick virtue; now then lightly speaking and wantonly acting, thereby to bear slander from all under Heaven—is very much not a plan.
translations.idiomatic: You should be observing the statutes and sending tribute without fail, day and night, to show gratitude for such patience. Instead you speak rashly and act as you please, inviting the censure of the whole realm. That is no way to secure your position.

sentences.zh: 夫大王以千里為宅居，以萬民為臣妾，此高皇帝之厚德也。

translations.literal: Now the Great King takes a thousand li as his dwelling and the myriad people as his concubines and servants—this is the thick virtue of the Supreme Emperor.
translations.idiomatic: You hold a thousand li as your demesne and count tens of thousands as your subjects—that is the legacy the Gaozu Emperor bequeathed you out of deep kindness.

sentences.zh: 高帝蒙霜露，沫風雨，赴矢石，野戰攻城，身被創痍，以為子孫成萬世之業，艱難危苦甚矣，大王不思先帝之艱苦，日夜怵惕，修身正行，養犧牲，豐潔粢盛，奉祭祀，以無忘先帝之功德，而欲屬國為布衣，甚過。

translations.literal: The Supreme Emperor endured frost and dew, licked wind and rain, went to arrows and stones, fought in the field and assaulted cities, his body bearing wounds and scars, in order for sons and grandsons to complete the enterprise of ten thousand generations—the hardship and peril and bitterness were extreme; the Great King does not ponder the Supreme Emperor's hardship, day and night fearful and cautious, cultivating the person and correcting conduct, raising sacrificial victims, making abundant and pure grain offerings, serving sacrifices, thereby not to forget the Supreme Emperor's merit and virtue, yet wishes to hand over the state and be a commoner—very excessive.
translations.idiomatic: The Gaozu Emperor braved wind and weather, faced arrows and siege engines, and bore scars on his body to win an empire that would endure for your line. The cost was beyond measure. You ought to tremble day and night, mend your conduct, fatten the sacrificial beasts, prepare spotless offerings, and keep his shrines bright in memory of what he did. To throw away your throne for a commoner's life is to betray that sacrifice in the gravest way.

sentences.zh: 且夫貪讓國土之名，輕廢先帝之業，不可以言孝。

translations.literal: Moreover coveting the name of yielding state territory, lightly casting aside the Supreme Emperor's enterprise—one cannot call this filial.
translations.idiomatic: To covet the reputation of humility while casting aside what your father built is not filial piety by any measure.

sentences.zh: 父為之基，而不能守，不賢。

translations.literal: The father made for him the foundation, yet he cannot guard it—not worthy.
translations.idiomatic: Your father laid the foundation; you cannot hold it—that is not the conduct of a worthy man.

sentences.zh: 不求守長陵，而求之真定，先母后父，不誼。

translations.literal: Not seeking to guard Changling, yet seeking it at Zhending—mother first, father after—not right.
translations.idiomatic: You would not stay to guard your father's tomb at Changling but insist on Zhending for your mother's sake—putting mother before father is contrary to right principle.

sentences.zh: 數逆天子之令，不順。

translations.literal: Repeatedly opposing the Son of Heaven's commands—not compliant.
translations.idiomatic: Time and again you have defied the emperor's orders—that is disloyalty.

sentences.zh: 言節行以高兄，無禮。

translations.literal: Speaking of integrity of conduct to elevate the elder brother—without ritual.
translations.idiomatic: You parade your moral outrage to shame your elder brother—that is rank discourtesy.

sentences.zh: 幸臣有罪，大者立斷，小者肉刑，不仁。

translations.literal: Favored ministers having guilt, great ones immediately cut off, small ones mutilating punishment—not benevolent.
translations.idiomatic: When a favorite commits a crime, you execute him on the spot if the offense is grave or mutilate him if it is slight—such cruelty is the opposite of benevolence.

sentences.zh: 貴布衣一劍之任，賤王侯之位，不知。

translations.literal: Honoring the single-sword duty of a commoner, despising the position of kings and marquises—not knowing.
translations.idiomatic: You prize the bravado of a swordsman in plain cloth and scorn the station of a prince—that is folly, not wisdom.

sentences.zh: 不好學問大道，觸情忘行，不祥。

translations.literal: Not liking to study and inquire into the great Way, touching emotion and forgetting conduct—not auspicious.
translations.idiomatic: You spurn learning and the great principles, follow every impulse, and forget how a ruler should act—such conduct invites misfortune.

sentences.zh: 此八者，危亡之路也，而大王行之，棄南面之位，奮諸、賁之勇，常出入危亡之路，臣之所見，高皇帝之神必不廟食於大王之手，明白。

translations.literal: These eight are roads of peril and destruction, yet the Great King walks them, casting aside the position of facing south, rousing the bravery of Zhu and Ben, constantly going out and in on roads of peril and destruction—as your servant sees it, the spirit of the Supreme Emperor will surely not receive temple offerings from the Great King's hands—clear and bright.
translations.idiomatic: These eight faults are the path to ruin, yet you pursue them: you abandon your throne to play the hero like Zhu Hai or Meng Ben, and you tread the brink of disaster at every turn. In my view the spirit of the Gaozu Emperor will never accept sacrifice from your house if you continue on this course—that much is plain.

sentences.zh: 昔者，周公誅管叔，放蔡叔，以安周；

translations.literal: In former times, the Duke of Zhou executed Guan Shu and banished Cai Shu, thereby to pacify Zhou;
translations.idiomatic: In antiquity the Duke of Zhou executed his brother Guan Shu and banished Cai Shu to secure the Zhou house;

sentences.zh: 齊桓殺其弟，以反國；

translations.literal: Duke Huan of Qi killed his younger brother, thereby to return to the state;
translations.idiomatic: Duke Huan of Qi put his brother to death in order to regain his throne;

sentences.zh: 秦始皇殺兩弟，遷其母，以安秦；

translations.literal: The First Emperor of Qin killed two younger brothers, banished his mother, thereby to pacify Qin;
translations.idiomatic: The First Emperor killed two rebellious brothers and removed his mother from court to stabilize Qin;

sentences.zh: 頃王亡代，高帝奪之國，以便事；

translations.literal: King Qing lost Dai; the Supreme Emperor seized his state, thereby to facilitate affairs;
translations.idiomatic: When the king of Dai lost his realm, Gaozu annexed it for the good of the realm;

sentences.zh: 濟北舉兵，皇帝誅之，以安漢。

translations.literal: The King of Jinbei raised troops; the Emperor executed him, thereby to pacify Han.
translations.idiomatic: When the king of Jinbei rose in arms, the emperor struck him down to restore peace to Han.

sentences.zh: 故周、齊行之於古，秦、漢用之於今，大王不察古今之所以安國便事，而欲以親戚之意望於太上，不可得也。

translations.literal: Therefore Zhou and Qi practiced it in antiquity, Qin and Han employ it in the present; the Great King does not examine how antiquity and the present thereby pacify the state and facilitate affairs, yet wishes by kin feeling's intent to look to the Grand Supreme—cannot be obtained.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou and Qi did as much in the old days; Qin and Han do the same today. You ignore how rulers past and present secure their states, yet imagine that blood ties alone will move the sovereign to spare you—that is a vain hope.

sentences.zh: 亡之諸侯，游宦事人，及捨匿者，論皆有法。

translations.literal: Fugitive feudal lords, wandering officials serving others, and those who harbor and conceal—all are judged by statute.
translations.idiomatic: Deserted nobles, wandering retainers in others' service, and anyone who harbors them are all covered by explicit law.

sentences.zh: 其在王所，吏主者坐。

translations.literal: If they are at the king's place, the supervising official is held liable.
translations.idiomatic: When such men turn up on a prince's lands, the officials in charge bear the penalty.

sentences.zh: 今諸侯子為吏者，御史主；

translations.literal: Now for sons of feudal lords who become clerks, the imperial clerk supervises;
translations.idiomatic: Sons of nobles who take office as clerks answer to the imperial clerk;

sentences.zh: 為軍吏者，中尉主；

translations.literal: those who become military clerks, the commandant of the capital supervises;
translations.idiomatic: those who serve as military officers, to the commandant of the capital;

sentences.zh: 客出入殿門者，衛尉大行主；

translations.literal: guests who go in and out of the palace hall gates, the commandant of the guards and the grand messenger supervise;
translations.idiomatic: guests who pass the palace gates fall under the commandant of the guards and the grand messenger;

sentences.zh: 諸從蠻夷來歸誼及以亡名數自佔者，內史縣令主。

translations.literal: all who follow barbarians coming to submit in good faith and those who by flight from registered names self-register—the imperial secretary and county magistrates supervise.
translations.idiomatic: tribesmen who come to offer allegiance and fugitives who surrender themselves are registered by the metropolitan governor and county magistrates.

sentences.zh: 相欲委下吏，無與其禍，不可得也。

translations.literal: If the chancellor wishes to shift blame to lower clerks and not share in the calamity—cannot be obtained.
translations.idiomatic: Your chancellor cannot shift the blame downward and hope to escape the consequences himself.

sentences.zh: 王若不改，漢系大王邸，論相以下，為之奈何？

translations.literal: If the king does not reform, Han will bind the Great King in his mansion and prosecute from the chancellor downward—what to do about it?
translations.idiomatic: If you do not mend your ways, the court will seal your residence in the capital and prosecute every official from your chancellor on down. What then?

sentences.zh: 夫墮父大業，退為布衣所哀，幸臣皆伏法而誅，為天下笑，以羞先帝之德，甚為大王不取也。

translations.literal: Now to ruin the father's great enterprise, withdraw and be pitied by commoners, favored ministers all submit to law and are executed, be laughed at by all under Heaven, thereby to shame the Supreme Emperor's virtue—very much is this not to be taken by the Great King.
translations.idiomatic: To squander your father's achievement, sink until commoners pity you, watch your favorites led off to execution, and become a laughingstock before the empire would disgrace the memory of Gaozu. Surely that is a course you cannot intend.

sentences.zh: 宜急改操易行，上書謝罪，曰：「臣不幸早失先帝，少孤，呂氏之世，未嘗忘死。

translations.literal: You ought urgently to change conduct and alter practice, submit a memorial apologizing for guilt, saying, "Your servant unhappily early lost the Supreme Emperor, was young and orphaned; in the age of the Lü clan, never forgot death.
translations.idiomatic: You should change course at once and memorialize the throne in words such as these: "I was orphaned while still a boy and lived every day of the Lü regime expecting execution.

sentences.zh: 陛下即位，臣怙恩德驕盈，行多不軌。

translations.literal: When Your Majesty ascended the throne, your servant relied on favor and virtue, was arrogant and overflowing, conduct much not in the track.
translations.idiomatic: Since Your Majesty took the throne I have presumed on your kindness, grown arrogant, and repeatedly overstepped what the law allows.

sentences.zh: 追念罪過，恐懼，伏地待誅不敢起。」

translations.literal: Reflecting on crimes and faults, fearful, prostrate on the ground awaiting execution, not daring to rise."
translations.idiomatic: When I think of my offenses I tremble; I lie prostrate and await death, not daring to rise.

sentences.zh: 皇帝聞之必喜。

translations.literal: When the Emperor hears it he will surely be pleased.
translations.idiomatic: Such words would please the emperor.

sentences.zh: 大王昆弟歡欣於上，群臣皆得延壽於上；

translations.literal: The Great King's fraternal brethren will be joyful above; the host of ministers all will obtain extended life above;
translations.idiomatic: You and your brother would be reconciled in the Son of Heaven's sight, and every minister could look to a long career at court;

sentences.zh: 上下得宜，海內常安。

translations.literal: above and below obtaining what is fitting, within the seas constantly at peace.
translations.idiomatic: high and low would be at ease, and the realm would know lasting calm.

sentences.zh: 願孰計而疾行之。

translations.literal: I hope you will maturely plan and swiftly practice it.
translations.idiomatic: Weigh this carefully and act on it without delay.

sentences.zh: 行之有疑，禍如發矢，不可追已。

translations.literal: If in practicing it there is hesitation, calamity is like loosing an arrow—cannot be pursued afterward.
translations.idiomatic: Hesitate, and disaster will fly like a loosed arrow—nothing can call it back.

sentences.zh: 王得書不說。

translations.literal: The king received the letter and was not pleased.
translations.idiomatic: The king read the letter and took no pleasure in it.

sentences.zh: 六年，令男子但等七十人與棘蒲侯柴武太子奇謀，以輦車四十乘反谷口，令人使閩越、匈奴。

translations.literal: In the sixth year, he ordered the man Dan and others, seventy persons, together with Qi, heir of Marquis Chai Wu of Jipu, to plot, using forty convoy wagons to rebel at Gukou, and sent men to envoy Minyue and the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: In the sixth year of Wen's reign he had seventy men under a certain Dan join Qi, son of Marquis Chai Wu of Jipu, in a plot to seize Gukou with forty wagonloads of followers and to send envoys to Minyue and the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 事覺，治之，乃使使召淮南王。

translations.literal: The affair was discovered; they prosecuted it; thereupon they sent envoys to summon the King of Huainan.
translations.idiomatic: When the plot came to light and the principals were arrested, the court sent messengers to summon the king of Huainan to the capital.

sentences.zh: 王至長安，丞相張蒼，典客馮敬行御史大夫事，與宗正、廷尉雜奏：「長廢先帝法，不聽天子詔，居處無度，為黃屋蓋擬天子，擅為法令，不用法令。

translations.literal: When the king arrived at Chang'an, Chancellor Zhang Cang, the protocol secretary Feng Jing acting the office of imperial counselor, together with the director of the imperial clan and the commandant of justice jointly memorialized: "Chang casts aside the Supreme Emperor's laws, does not heed the Son of Heaven's edicts, dwelling and deportment without measure, used a yellow canopy emulating the Son of Heaven, on his own authority made laws and orders, does not use the laws and orders."
translations.idiomatic: When Liu Chang reached Chang'an, Chancellor Zhang Cang, Feng Jing of the office for guests (acting as imperial counselor), the director of the imperial clan, and the commandant of justice filed a joint indictment: "Liu Chang has spurned the laws left by the late emperor, defied imperial edicts, and lived without restraint. He has used the yellow imperial canopy, issued laws on his own authority, and set aside the statutes of Han."

sentences.zh: 及所置吏，以其郎中春為丞相，收聚漢諸侯人及有罪亡者，匿為居，為治家室，賜與財物、爵祿、田宅，爵或至關內侯，奉以二千石所當得。

translations.literal: Moreover among officials he appointed, taking his gentleman-of-the-palace Chun as chancellor, he gathered Han feudal lords' people and those guilty who had fled, concealed them as residents, for them built houses and chambers, bestowed goods and wealth, ranks and salaries, fields and dwellings; ranks sometimes reaching marquis within the passes, supplying them with what two-thousand-dan officials ought to receive.
translations.idiomatic: Among his appointees he made his retainer Chun chancellor, harbored runaways and criminals from other nobles' households, hid them in his domain, built them houses, and showered them with wealth, titles, fields, and stipends—some to the rank of marquis within the passes, all on the scale reserved for two-thousand-dan officials of the court.

sentences.zh: 大夫但、士伍開章等七十人與棘蒲侯太子奇謀反，欲以危宗廟社稷，謀使閩越及匈奴發其兵。

translations.literal: Grandee Dan, common soldier Kaizhang and others seventy persons together with the heir of Marquis Jipu Qi plotted rebellion, intending thereby to endanger the ancestral temple and altars of soil and grain, plotting to cause Minyue and the Xiongnu to raise their troops.
translations.idiomatic: Grandee Dan, the discharged soldier Kaizhang, and some seventy confederates conspired with Qi, heir of Marquis Chai Wu of Jipu, to subvert the altars of state and to rouse Minyue and the Xiongnu to arms.

sentences.zh: 事覺，長安尉奇等往捕開章，長匿不予，與故中尉□忌謀，殺以閉口，為棺槨衣衾，葬之肥陵，謾吏曰『不知安在』。

translations.literal: When the affair was discovered, the Chang'an commandant Qi and others went to arrest Kaizhang; Chang concealed and did not hand him over, plotted with the former commandant [missing graph] Ji, killed him to close mouths, made coffin and outer coffin, shroud and quilt, buried him at Feiling, deceived the clerks saying "do not know where he is."
translations.idiomatic: When the plot was uncovered, the Chang'an commandant Qi went to seize Kaizhang, but the king hid him and refused to surrender him. He then plotted with his former commandant [name missing] Ji to murder Kaizhang and silence him, gave him a full burial at Feiling, and told the officials he had no idea where the man had gone.

sentences.zh: 又陽聚土，樹表其上曰『開章死，葬此下』。

translations.literal: Moreover falsely he piled earth, erected a marker on it saying "Kaizhang died, buried below this."
translations.idiomatic: He then piled up a mound of earth and set a post on top falsely declaring, "Kaizhang is dead and lies buried beneath this spot.

sentences.zh: 及長身自賊殺無罪者一人；

translations.literal: Moreover Chang personally murdered by violence one guiltless person;
translations.idiomatic: He personally murdered one innocent man with his own hands.

sentences.zh: 令吏論殺無罪者六人；

translations.literal: he ordered clerks to judge and kill six guiltless persons;
translations.idiomatic: He ordered his officials to condemn and execute six more innocents.

sentences.zh: 為亡命棄市詐捕命者以除罪；

translations.literal: for fugitives under sentence of execution in the market he falsely arrested others who were wanted, thereby to remove their guilt;
translations.idiomatic: For men facing execution as fugitives, he staged sham roundups of other wanted men so their capital crimes could be wiped away.

sentences.zh: 擅罪人，無告劾繫治城旦以上十四人；

translations.literal: he on his own authority imprisoned men, without accusation or impeachment bound and treated fourteen persons at hard labor dawn watch and above;
translations.idiomatic: Without indictment he jailed fourteen men and sentenced them to hard labor or worse.

sentences.zh: 赦免罪人死罪十八人，城旦春以下五十八人；

translations.literal: he pardoned and released convicts of capital crime eighteen persons, of hard labor dawn and spring pounding and below fifty-eight persons;
translations.idiomatic: He pardoned eighteen men condemned to death and fifty-eight serving lesser hard-labor sentences.

sentences.zh: 賜人爵關內侯以下九十四人。

translations.literal: he bestowed on people ranks from marquis within the passes downward ninety-four persons.
translations.idiomatic: He ennobled ninety-four followers with ranks as high as marquis within the passes.

sentences.zh: 前日長病，陛下心憂之，使使者賜棗脯，長不肯見拜使者。

translations.literal: The other day Chang was ill; Your Majesty's heart worried for him, sent envoys to bestow jujubes and dried meat; Chang was unwilling to appear and bow to the envoys.
translations.idiomatic: When he fell ill not long ago Your Majesty sent him dates and dried meat by messenger, yet he refused to receive the envoys or acknowledge the gift.

sentences.zh: 南海民處廬江界中者反，淮南吏卒擊之。

translations.literal: People of Nanhai dwelling within the border of Lujiang rebelled; Huainan clerks and soldiers struck them.
translations.idiomatic: When Nanhai tribesmen living along the Lujiang frontier rose in revolt, he sent Huainan troops against them.

sentences.zh: 陛下遣使者繼帛五千匹，以賜吏卒勞苦者。

translations.literal: Your Majesty dispatched envoys with five thousand bolts of silk to bestow on clerks and soldiers who had toiled in hardship.
translations.idiomatic: Your Majesty then sent five thousand bolts of silk to reward the officers and men who had borne the brunt of the fighting.

sentences.zh: 長不欲受賜，謾曰『無勞苦者』。

translations.literal: Chang did not wish to receive the gift, deceived saying "there are none who toiled in hardship."
translations.idiomatic: Liu Chang would not accept the bounty and lied that no one deserved a reward for hardship.

sentences.zh: 南海王織上書獻璧帛皇帝，忌擅燔其書，不以聞。

translations.literal: The King of Nanhai Zhi submitted a memorial presenting jade disks and silks to the Emperor; Ji on his own authority burned his memorial, did not report it.
translations.idiomatic: When the king of Nanhai, Zhi, memorialized the throne offering jade and silk, a man named Ji seized the document and burned it without informing the court.

sentences.zh: 吏請召治忌，長不遣，謾曰『忌病』。

translations.literal: Clerks requested to summon and prosecute Ji; Chang did not send him, deceived saying "Ji is ill."
translations.idiomatic: When officials asked to arrest Ji for questioning, the king refused to hand him over and claimed he was too ill to travel.

sentences.zh: 長所犯不軌，當棄市，臣請論如法」。

translations.literal: What Chang has committed is not in the track; he ought to be executed in the market; your servants request to judge according to law."
translations.idiomatic: The offenses Liu Chang has committed lie outside the bounds of the law; he deserves execution in the public market. We ask that he be sentenced accordingly.

sentences.zh: 制曰：「朕不忍置法於王，其與列侯、吏二千石議。」

translations.literal: The edict said: "We cannot bear to apply the law to the king; let him consult with full marquises and officials of two thousand dan."
translations.idiomatic: The emperor replied, "I cannot bring myself to judge my brother by the full rigor of the law. Let the marquises and senior officials deliberate."

sentences.zh: 列侯、吏二千石臣嬰等四十三人議，皆曰：「宜論如法。」

translations.literal: Full marquises and officials of two thousand dan, ministers Ying and others forty-three persons deliberated, all saying: "Ought to be judged according to law."
translations.idiomatic: Forty-three marquises and two-thousand-dan officials, led by Zhou Ying, unanimously recommended that the statutes be applied as written.

sentences.zh: 制曰：「其赦長死罪，廢勿王。」

translations.literal: The edict said: "Pardon Chang's capital crime; depose him, do not let him be king."
translations.idiomatic: The emperor decreed, "Spare Liu Chang execution, but strip him of his kingdom and his title."

sentences.zh: 有司奏：「請處蜀嚴道邛郵，遣其子、子母從居，縣為築蓋家室，皆日三食，給薪菜鹽炊食器席蓐。」

translations.literal: The responsible officials memorialized: "We request to place him at the Qiongyi post in Yandao, Shu, send his sons and the mothers of his sons to follow and dwell with him, have the county build houses and roofs for them, all three meals per day, and supply firewood, vegetables, salt, cooking gear, mats, and bedding."
translations.idiomatic: The ministry proposed banishing him to the Qiongyi relay in Yandao, Shu, allowing his sons and their mothers to go with him, ordering the county to erect proper quarters, provide three meals a day, and issue fuel, rations, salt, utensils, and bedding.

sentences.zh: 制曰：』食長，給肉日五斤，酒二斗。

translations.literal: The edict said: "Feed Chang; supply meat five jin a day, wine two dou."
translations.idiomatic: The edict added, "See that he is fed generously—five jin of meat and two dou of wine each day."

sentences.zh: 令故美人、材人得幸者十人從居。」

translations.literal: Order ten former beauties and talented women who had received favor to follow residence."
translations.idiomatic: Let ten of his former concubines and palace women who once shared his favor go with him into exile.

sentences.zh: 於是盡誅所與謀者。

translations.literal: Thereupon they executed to the last all who had plotted with him.
translations.idiomatic: Every man implicated in the conspiracy was then put to death.

sentences.zh: 乃遣長，載以輜車，令縣次傳。

translations.literal: Thereupon they sent off Chang, carrying him in a curtained cart, ordering counties to relay in sequence.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Chang was placed in a sealed prison wagon and sent west under escort, relayed from county to county along the route.

sentences.zh: 爰盎諫曰：「上素驕淮南王，不為置嚴相傅，以故至此。

translations.literal: Yuan Ang remonstrated, saying, "The Supreme One has always indulged the King of Huainan, not for him appointed a strict chancellor and tutor; by this cause arrived here."
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Ang urged him: "You have always spoiled the king of Huainan and never gave him a stern chancellor or tutor. That is how things have come to this pass."

sentences.zh: 且淮南王為人剛，今暴摧折之，臣恐其逢霧露病死，陛下有殺弟之名，奈何！」

translations.literal: Moreover the King of Huainan by nature is rigid; now violently crushing and breaking him, your servant fears he may encounter mist and dew and die of illness; Your Majesty will have the name of killing a younger brother—what to do!"
translations.idiomatic: Moreover he is a proud, unbending man. If you break him by force, I fear he will sicken and die on the road, and you will bear the stain of having murdered your own brother. What then?"

sentences.zh: 上曰：「吾特苦之耳，令復之。」

translations.literal: The Supreme One said, "I only meant to distress him; order to restore him."
translations.idiomatic: The emperor said, "I only meant to humble him a little. Send orders to ease his treatment."

sentences.zh: 淮南王謂侍者曰：「誰謂乃公勇者？

translations.literal: The King of Huainan told his attendants, saying, "Who says your lord father is brave?
translations.idiomatic: In the cart Liu Chang said to his attendants, "Who ever called your old master a brave man?

sentences.zh: 吾以驕不聞過，故至此。」

translations.literal: I because arrogant did not hear of faults; therefore arrived here."
translations.idiomatic: I grew arrogant and would hear no criticism of myself—that is how I have come to this."

sentences.zh: 乃不食而死。

translations.literal: Thereupon he did not eat and died.
translations.idiomatic: He refused all food and starved himself to death.

sentences.zh: 縣傳者不敢發車封。

translations.literal: Those who relayed by county did not dare open the cart seals.
translations.idiomatic: At each relay station along the route the escorts dared not break the seals on his wagon.

sentences.zh: 至雍，雍令發之，以死聞。

translations.literal: When they reached Yong, the magistrate of Yong opened it and reported by death.
translations.idiomatic: When the procession reached Yong, the local magistrate opened the wagon and reported that the prisoner was dead.

sentences.zh: 上悲哭，謂爰盎曰：「吾不從公言，卒亡淮南王。」

translations.literal: The Supreme One wept in grief and told Yuan Ang, "I did not follow your words; in the end lost the King of Huainan."
translations.idiomatic: The emperor wept and said to Yuan Ang, "I did not heed your warning, and now my brother of Huainan is gone."

sentences.zh: 盎曰：「淮南王不可奈何，願陛下自寬。」

translations.literal: Ang said, "The King of Huainan cannot be helped; I hope Your Majesty will himself be broad-minded."
translations.idiomatic: Ang replied, "Nothing can bring him back now, Your Majesty. You must forgive yourself."

sentences.zh: 上曰：「為之奈何？」

translations.literal: The Supreme One said, "What to do about it?"
translations.idiomatic: The emperor asked, "What am I to do?"

sentences.zh: 曰：「獨斬丞相、御史以謝天下乃可。」

translations.literal: He said, "Only beheading the chancellor and the imperial counselor to apologize to all under Heaven then can do."
translations.idiomatic: Ang answered, "Only if you execute the chancellor and the imperial counselor and offer their heads to the empire can you appease the people."

sentences.zh: 上即令丞相、御史逮諸縣傳淮南王不發封饋侍者，皆棄市，乃以列侯葬淮南王於雍，置守塚三十家。

translations.literal: The Supreme One immediately ordered the chancellor and imperial counselor to seize all those in the counties who relayed the King of Huainan without opening the seals and who fed the attendants—all executed in the market; thereupon with full marquis rites buried the King of Huainan at Yong, establishing thirty households to guard the tomb.
translations.idiomatic: He ordered the arrest of every county officer who had relayed the king without breaking the wagon seals and of everyone who had slipped food to his attendants; all were executed in the marketplace. Liu Chang was then buried at Yong with the honors due a full marquis, and thirty families were assigned to tend his grave.

sentences.zh: 孝文八年，憐淮南王，王有子四人，年皆七八歲，乃封子安為阜陵侯，子勃為安陽侯，子賜為陽周侯，子良為東城侯。

translations.literal: In the eighth year of Emperor Xiaowen, pitying the King of Huainan, the king having four sons, all aged seven or eight sui, thereupon enfeoffed the son An as marquis of Fuling, the son Bo as marquis of Anyang, the son Ci as marquis of Yangzhou, the son Liang as marquis of Dongcheng.
translations.idiomatic: In 172 BCE, moved by pity for the late king of Huainan, the emperor enfeoffed his four young sons—each seven or eight years old—as marquises: Liu An at Fuling, Liu Bo at Anyang, Liu Ci at Yangzhou, and Liu Liang at Dongcheng.

sentences.zh: 十二年，民有作歌歌淮南王曰：「一尺布，尚可縫；

translations.literal: In the twelfth year, the people had a song made singing the King of Huainan, saying, "One foot of cloth, still can be sewn;
translations.idiomatic: In 168 BCE a ballad circulated about the king of Huainan: "A foot of cloth can still be stitched together;

sentences.zh: 一斗粟，尚可春。

translations.literal: one dou of grain, still can be pounded."
translations.idiomatic: a peck of grain can still be ground in the mortar;"

sentences.zh: 兄弟二人，不相容！」

translations.literal: two brothers in one house, not tolerating each other!"
translations.idiomatic: yet two brothers under one roof cannot live in peace!"

sentences.zh: 上聞之曰，昔堯、舜放逐骨肉，周公殺管、蔡，天下稱聖，不以私害公。

translations.literal: When the Supreme One heard it he said, in former times Yao and Shun banished bone and flesh, the Duke of Zhou killed Guan and Cai; all under Heaven called them sage, not by private harming public.
translations.idiomatic: When the emperor heard the song he observed that Yao and Shun had banished their kin, and the Duke of Zhou had executed Guan and Cai, yet the world hailed them as sages because they put the common good above private feeling.

sentences.zh: 天下豈以為我貪淮南地邪！」

translations.literal: Would all under Heaven think I covet the land of Huainan!"
translations.idiomatic: Surely no one imagines I murdered my brother for the sake of Huainan!"

sentences.zh: 乃徙城陽王王淮南故地，而追尊謚淮南王為厲王，置園如諸侯儀。

translations.literal: Thereupon he moved the King of Chengyang to be king in the former territory of Huainan, and posthumously honored with the posthumous title Prince Li the King of Huainan, establishing a park like the rites of feudal lords.
translations.idiomatic: He therefore transferred the king of Chengyang to the old Huainan domain, posthumously titled his late brother the Prince Li of Huainan, and laid out a memorial park for him with the ceremonies due a feudal lord.

sentences.zh: 十六年，上憐淮南王廢法不軌，自使失國早夭，乃徙淮南王喜復王故城陽，而立厲王三子王淮南故地，三分之：阜陵侯安為淮南王，安陽侯勃為衡山王，陽周侯賜為廬江王，東城侯良前薨，無後。

translations.literal: In the sixteenth year, the Supreme One pitied the King of Huainan for casting aside law and not being in the track, himself causing loss of state and early death; thereupon moved the King of Huainan Xi again to be king of former Chengyang, and established the three sons of Prince Li as kings in the former territory of Huainan, dividing it in three: the Marquis of Fuling An as King of Huainan, the Marquis of Anyang Bo as King of Hengshan, the Marquis of Yangzhou Ci as King of Lujiang; the Marquis of Dongcheng Liang had earlier died, without posterity.
translations.idiomatic: In 164 BCE the emperor grieved that his brother had lost his life through lawlessness and an early fall from power. He moved Liu Xi, the current king of Huainan, back to Chengyang and divided the old Huainan lands among Prince Li's three surviving sons: Liu An became king of Huainan, Liu Bo king of Hengshan, and Liu Ci king of Lujiang. The fourth son, Marquis Liu Liang of Dongcheng, had already died without issue.

sentences.zh: 淮南王安為人好書，鼓琴，不喜戈獵狗馬馳騁，亦欲以行陰德拊循百姓，流名譽。

translations.literal: The King of Huainan An by nature loved books, strummed the zither, did not like spears hunting dogs horses galloping, also wished by practicing hidden virtue to soothe the hundred surnames, spreading name and reputation.
translations.idiomatic: Liu An, king of Huainan, loved learning and the zither, cared little for hunting or racing horses and hounds, and hoped to win the people's hearts by quiet acts of kindness and so build a name for benevolence.

sentences.zh: 招致賓客方術之士數千人，作為《內書》二十一篇，《外書》甚眾，又有《中篇》八卷，言神仙黃白之術，亦二十餘萬言。

translations.literal: He summoned guests and masters of formulas and techniques numbering several thousand, making the Inner Documents in twenty-one chapters, the Outer Documents very numerous, also having the Middle Pieces in eight scrolls, speaking of transcendents, elixirs, and yellow-white techniques—also more than two hundred thousand graphs.
translations.idiomatic: He gathered several thousand retainers and adepts of esoteric arts, produced twenty-one chapters of the Inner Writings and a vast body of Outer Writings, plus eight scrolls of the Middle Treatise on immortals and the alchemy of gold—all told, well over two hundred thousand characters.

sentences.zh: 時武帝方好藝文，以安屬為諸父，辯博善為文辭，甚尊重之。

translations.literal: At that time Emperor Wu was just then fond of arts and letters; because An belonged to the class of fathers' generation, was eloquent and broad and good at literary phrasing, he greatly honored and respected him.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wu was cultivating literature and the arts; Liu An, as an uncle on his father's side, was learned, eloquent, and a fine writer, and the emperor treated him with marked respect.

sentences.zh: 每為報書及賜，常召司馬相如等視草乃遣。

translations.literal: Whenever replying to letters and bestowals, he constantly summoned Sima Xiangru and others to review the draft before dispatching.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever he drafted replies to the throne or imperial gifts, the emperor would often call in Sima Xiangru and others to polish the text before it was sent.

sentences.zh: 初，安入朝，獻所作《內篇》，新出，上愛秘之。

translations.literal: At first, when An entered court audience, he presented what he had made, the Inner Chapters, newly issued; the Supreme One loved and kept them secret.
translations.idiomatic: On Liu An's first visit to court he presented his newly finished Inner Chapters; the emperor prized them and had them locked away in the palace library.

sentences.zh: 使為《離騷傳》，旦受詔，日食時上。

translations.literal: He ordered him to make a Commentary on Li sao; at dawn receiving the edict, by the time of the sun meal he presented it.
translations.idiomatic: He commissioned a commentary on the Li sao; Liu An received the order at dawn and presented the finished work by breakfast.

sentences.zh: 又獻《頌德》及《長安都國頌》。

translations.literal: He also presented In Praise of Virtue and In Praise of the Metropolis and States of Chang'an.
translations.idiomatic: He also offered his Hymn in Praise of Virtue and his Ode to Chang'an and the Commanderies and Kingdoms.

sentences.zh: 每宴見，談說得失及方技賦頌，昏莫然後罷。

translations.literal: Whenever feasting audience, discussing right and wrong and formulas, techniques, rhapsodies, and hymns, at dusk only then ceased.
translations.idiomatic: At each informal audience they would debate policy, literature, and occult lore until dusk before breaking off.

sentences.zh: 安初入朝，雅善太尉武安侯，武安侯迎之霸上，與語曰：「方今上無太子，王親高皇帝孫，行仁義，天下莫不聞。

translations.literal: When An first entered court audience, he was on good terms with the Grand Commandant Marquis of Wu'an; the Marquis of Wu'an met him at Bashang and spoke with him, saying, "At present the Supreme One has no heir apparent; the king is in person a grandson of the Supreme Emperor, practices benevolence and right—none under Heaven has not heard."
translations.idiomatic: On Liu An's first visit to the capital he struck up a friendship with Tian Fen, the Marquis of Wu'an, who met him at Bashang and said, "The emperor has no heir. You are a grandson of the Gaozu Emperor in the direct line, and your reputation for benevolence is known everywhere."

sentences.zh: 宮車一日晏駕，非王尚誰立者！」

translations.literal: When the palace carriage one day goes on the late drive, if not the king, who else is to be established!"
translations.idiomatic: When the imperial carriage one day makes its final journey, who else should succeed but you?"

sentences.zh: 淮南王大喜，厚遺武安侯寶賂。

translations.literal: The King of Huainan was greatly pleased and richly presented the Marquis of Wu'an with treasures and bribes.
translations.idiomatic: Liu An was overjoyed and showered the Marquis of Wu'an with rich gifts.

sentences.zh: 其群臣賓客，江淮間多輕薄，以厲王遷死感激安。

translations.literal: His host of ministers and guests—between the Yangzi and Huai many were frivolous—because Prince Li had died in banishment, were stirred in sympathy toward An.
translations.idiomatic: Many of his retainers between the Yangzi and the Huai were reckless men who still burned with indignation over Prince Li's death on the road to exile and fed Liu An's ambitions.

sentences.zh: 建元六年，彗星見，淮南王心怪之。

translations.literal: In the sixth year of Jianyuan, a comet appeared; the King of Huainan in his heart thought it strange.
translations.idiomatic: In 135 BCE a comet appeared, and the king of Huainan took it for an omen.

sentences.zh: 或說王曰：「先吳軍時，彗星出，長數尺，然尚流血千里。

translations.literal: Someone persuaded the king, saying, "Before the Wu campaign, a comet appeared, several feet long, yet still blood flowed a thousand li.
translations.idiomatic: A retainer told him, "Before the revolt of Wu, the comet was only a few feet long, yet still the fighting bloodied the empire for a thousand li.

sentences.zh: 今彗星竟天，天下兵當大起。」

translations.literal: Now the comet fills the sky; arms under Heaven ought greatly to rise."
translations.idiomatic: This one stretches across the whole sky—great war must be coming."

sentences.zh: 王心以為上無太子，天下有變，諸侯並爭，愈益治攻戰具，積金錢賂遺郡國。

translations.literal: The king in his heart thought that because the Supreme One had no heir apparent, if all under Heaven had change, the feudal lords together would contend; he increasingly prepared tools for attack and war, piled gold and cash to bribe and gift commanderies and kingdoms.
translations.idiomatic: Convinced that the lack of an heir would throw the realm into turmoil among the princes, he stepped up the manufacture of weapons, hoarded gold, and sent bribes to officials in commanderies and kingdoms across the empire.

sentences.zh: 游士妄作妖言阿諛王，王喜，多賜予之。

translations.literal: Wandering scholars recklessly made prodigious words flattering the king; the king was pleased and much bestowed on them.
translations.idiomatic: Itinerant scholars fed him wild prophecies and flattery; pleased, he rewarded them handsomely.

sentences.zh: 王有女陵，彗有口。

translations.literal: The king had a daughter Ling; comet had a mouth.
translations.idiomatic: The king had a daughter named Ling, sharp-tongued as a comet's tail.

sentences.zh: 王愛陵，多予金錢，為中□長安，約結上左右。

translations.literal: The king loved Ling, much gave her gold and cash, for her to dwell in Chang'an, covenanting and knotting those to the left and right of the Supreme One.
translations.idiomatic: He doted on her, supplied her generously with cash, and stationed her in Chang'an to buy friends at court.

sentences.zh: 元朔二年，上賜淮南王几杖，不朝。

translations.literal: In the second year of Yuanshuo, the Supreme One bestowed on the King of Huainan armrest and staff, not requiring court audience.
translations.idiomatic: In 127 BCE the emperor granted him a stool and staff of age, exempting him from the duty of attending court.

sentences.zh: 後荼愛幸，生子遷為太子，取皇太后外孫修成君女為太子妃。

translations.literal: Queen Tu received favor, bore a son Qian who became heir apparent; he took the daughter of the Empress Dowager's maternal grandson Lady Xiucheng as heir apparent's consort.
translations.idiomatic: His favored consort Tu bore a son, Liu Qian, whom he made crown prince. The prince took as his consort the daughter of Lady Xiucheng, a granddaughter of the empress dowager on the maternal side.

sentences.zh: 王謀為反具，畏太子妃知而內洩事，乃與太子謀，令詐不愛，三月不同席。

translations.literal: The king plotted to make tools for rebellion, feared the heir apparent's consort would know and inside leak the affair; thereupon plotted with the heir apparent, ordering him falsely not to love, three months not sharing the mat.
translations.idiomatic: Because he was preparing rebellion and feared the crown princess might learn of it and report to the palace, he plotted with the crown prince to feign estrangement: for three months the prince would not share his wife's bed.

sentences.zh: 王陽怒太子，閉使與妃同內，終不近妃。

translations.literal: The king feigned anger at the heir apparent, shut him making him with the consort in the same inner quarters, yet to the end did not approach the consort.
translations.idiomatic: The king pretended to punish the prince by confining him with his wife, yet the prince never touched her.

sentences.zh: 妃求去，王乃上書謝歸之。

translations.literal: The consort requested to depart; the king thereupon submitted a memorial apologizing and sending her back.
translations.idiomatic: When she begged leave to return home, the king memorialized the throne with an apology and sent her back to the capital.

sentences.zh: 後荼、太子遷及女陵擅國權，奪民田宅，妄致系人。

translations.literal: Queen Tu, the heir apparent Qian, and the daughter Ling usurped state power, seized the people's fields and dwellings, recklessly caused men to be bound.
translations.idiomatic: Queen Tu, Crown Prince Qian, and Princess Ling abused their power at court, seized commoners' land and houses, and threw innocent men into prison on whim.

sentences.zh: 太子學用劍，自以為人莫及，聞郎中雷被巧，召與戲，被壹再辭讓，誤中太子。

translations.literal: The heir apparent studied using the sword, considered himself unmatched by others; hearing that the gentleman Lei Bei was skillful, summoned him to fence; Bei repeatedly declined and yielded, by mistake struck the heir apparent.
translations.idiomatic: The crown prince had studied fencing and believed no one his equal. Hearing that the gentleman Lei Bei was a master of the blade, he summoned him to bout. Lei Bei declined again and again, but in the exchange he accidentally cut the prince.

sentences.zh: 太子怒，被恐。

translations.literal: The heir apparent was angry; Bei feared.
translations.idiomatic: The prince flew into a rage, and Lei Bei was terrified.

sentences.zh: 此時有欲從軍者輒詣長安，被即願奮擊匈奴。

translations.literal: At this time whoever wished to follow the army always went to Chang'an; Bei then wished to strive to strike the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: A general call had gone out for volunteers to report to Chang'an for service against the Xiongnu, and Lei Bei volunteered at once.

sentences.zh: 太子數惡被，王使郎中令斥免，欲以禁後。

translations.literal: The heir apparent repeatedly slandered Bei; the king ordered the supervisor of gentlemen to reprimand and dismiss him, wishing thereby to forbid what followed.
translations.idiomatic: The prince slandered Lei Bei repeatedly until the king had the supervisor of gentlemen dismiss him from office, hoping to deter him from leaving.

sentences.zh: 元朔五年，被遂亡之長安，上書自明。

translations.literal: In the fifth year of Yuanshuo, Bei thereupon fled to Chang'an and submitted a memorial to clear himself.
translations.idiomatic: In 124 BCE Lei Bei fled to the capital and memorialized the throne in his own defense.

sentences.zh: 事下廷尉、河南。

translations.literal: The affair was sent down to the commandant of justice and Henan.
translations.idiomatic: The case was referred to the commandant of justice and the Henan governor.

sentences.zh: 河南治，逮淮南太子，王、王后計欲毋遣太子，遂發兵。

translations.literal: Henan prosecuted, summoned the heir apparent of Huainan; the king and queen plotted wishing not to send the heir apparent, thereupon raised troops.
translations.idiomatic: When Henan ordered the crown prince brought in for questioning, the king and queen plotted to refuse and considered raising troops at once.

sentences.zh: 計未定，猶與十餘日。

translations.literal: The plan was not settled; they hesitated more than ten days.
translations.idiomatic: They wavered for more than ten days without reaching a decision.

sentences.zh: 會有詔即訊太子。

translations.literal: It happened that there was an edict to immediately question the heir apparent.
translations.idiomatic: Then an edict arrived ordering the crown prince to be examined on the spot.

sentences.zh: 淮南相怒壽春丞留太子逮不遣，劾不敬。

translations.literal: The chancellor of Huainan was angry that the assistant of Shouchun detained the heir apparent's summons and did not send him, impeached for irreverence.
translations.idiomatic: The chancellor of Huainan, furious that the assistant magistrate of Shouchun was holding the prince despite the writ of arrest, impeached him for disrespect.

sentences.zh: 王請相，相不聽。

translations.literal: The king requested the chancellor; the chancellor did not heed.
translations.idiomatic: The king appealed to the chancellor, who refused to yield.

sentences.zh: 王使人上書告相，事下廷尉治。

translations.literal: The king sent men to submit a memorial accusing the chancellor; the affair was sent down to the commandant of justice for prosecution.
translations.idiomatic: The king retaliated with a memorial accusing the chancellor, and the matter went to the commandant of justice.

sentences.zh: 從跡連王，王使人候司。

translations.literal: Following the traces connected the king; the king sent men to watch and spy.
translations.idiomatic: The investigation soon pointed toward the king himself, who posted spies to watch for the next move from the capital.

sentences.zh: 漢公卿請逮捕治王，王恐，欲發兵。

translations.literal: Han dukes and ministers requested to arrest and prosecute the king; the king feared, wished to raise troops.
translations.idiomatic: When the high ministers of Han demanded his arrest, panic seized him and he again thought of rising in arms.

sentences.zh: 太子遷謀曰：「漢使即逮王，令人衣衛士衣，持戟居王旁，有非是者，即刺殺之，臣亦使人刺殺淮南中尉，乃舉兵，未晚也。」

translations.literal: The heir apparent Qian plotted, saying, "When Han envoys come to seize the king, order men to wear guards' robes, hold halberds and stand by the king's side; whoever is not right, immediately stab and kill him; your servant also will send men to stab and kill the commandant of Huainan, then raise troops—not late."
translations.idiomatic: Crown Prince Qian proposed, "If Han envoys come to arrest you, Father, dress our men as palace guards and station them at your side with halberds. At the first wrong move, kill them. I will have our commandant of Huainan assassinated at the same moment, then we can rise—it will not be too late."

sentences.zh: 是時上不許公卿，而遣漢中尉宏即訊驗王。

translations.literal: At this time the Supreme One did not permit the dukes and ministers, but dispatched the Han commandant of the capital Hong to immediately question and examine the king.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor rejected the ministers' demand for arrest and instead sent the commandant of the capital, Hong, to question the king on the spot.

sentences.zh: 王視漢中尉顏色和，問斥雷被事耳，自度無何，不發。

translations.literal: The king observed the Han commandant's expression was mild, asked only about the matter of reprimanding Lei Bei; he measured himself as without issue, did not rise.
translations.idiomatic: Liu An saw that the commandant's manner was mild and that he asked only about the Lei Bei affair; reassured that nothing worse was afoot, he dropped the plan to revolt.

sentences.zh: 中尉還，以聞。

translations.literal: The commandant returned and reported.
translations.idiomatic: Hong returned to the capital and made his report.

sentences.zh: 公卿治者曰：「淮南王安雍閼求奮擊匈奴者雷被等，格明詔，當棄市。」

translations.literal: The prosecuting dukes and ministers said, "The King of Huainan An blocked and barred those who sought to strive to strike the Xiongnu such as Lei Bei, resisting the clear edict—ought to be executed in the market."
translations.idiomatic: The ministers ruled that Liu An had obstructed volunteers such as Lei Bei who wished to fight the Xiongnu, defying an explicit imperial order—a capital offense.

sentences.zh: 詔不許。

translations.literal: An edict did not permit.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor refused approval.

sentences.zh: 請廢勿王，上不許。

translations.literal: They requested to depose and not let him be king; the Supreme One did not permit.
translations.idiomatic: They asked that he be deposed entirely; again the emperor refused.

sentences.zh: 請削五縣，可二縣。

translations.literal: They requested to cut five counties; he approved two counties.
translations.idiomatic: They proposed stripping him of five counties; he allowed two to be taken.

sentences.zh: 使中尉宏赦其罪，罰以削地。

translations.literal: He ordered Commandant Hong to pardon his crime, punishing by cutting territory.
translations.idiomatic: He sent Commandant Hong to announce a pardon and commute the sentence to loss of territory.

sentences.zh: 中尉入淮南界，宣言赦王。

translations.literal: The commandant entered Huainan territory and publicly proclaimed pardon of the king.
translations.idiomatic: Hong crossed into Huainan and publicly proclaimed the king's pardon.

sentences.zh: 王初聞公卿請誅之，未知得削地，聞漢使來，恐其捕之，乃與太子謀如前計。

translations.literal: The king at first heard the dukes and ministers requested to execute him, did not yet know he would obtain cutting of territory; hearing Han envoys were coming, feared they would seize him; thereupon plotted with the heir apparent as the former plan.
translations.idiomatic: When Liu An first heard the ministers demand his death, before he learned that the sentence would be reduced to loss of fiefs, the approach of Han envoys convinced him he was to be arrested, and he revived the plot with his son.

sentences.zh: 中尉至，即賀王，王以故不發。

translations.literal: When the commandant arrived, immediately congratulated the king; the king for this reason did not rise.
translations.idiomatic: When Hong arrived he congratulated the king on his reprieve, and Liu An therefore did not rebel.

sentences.zh: 其後自傷曰：「吉行仁義見削地，寡人甚恥之。」

translations.literal: Afterward he wounded himself, saying, "I who practice benevolence and right see my territory cut—I am deeply shamed."
translations.idiomatic: Afterward he brooded, "I have walked the path of benevolence, yet I am punished with loss of land—this is a humiliation I cannot bear."

sentences.zh: 為反謀益甚。

translations.literal: His plotting for rebellion became more extreme.
translations.idiomatic: His rebellious designs grew more desperate than ever.

sentences.zh: 諸使者道長安來，為妄言，言上無男，即喜：言漢廷治，有男，即怒，以為妄言，非也。

translations.literal: The various envoys coming by way from Chang'an, he had them speak false words—if they said the Supreme One had no son, then he was pleased; if they said the Han court was orderly and had a son, then he was angry, considering it false words, not right.
translations.idiomatic: He coached envoys from the capital to bring him rumors: if they said the emperor had no son, he rejoiced; if they reported an orderly court and the birth of an heir, he flew into a rage and dismissed their words as lies.

sentences.zh: 日夜與左吳等按輿地圖，部署兵所從入。

translations.literal: Day and night with Zuo Wu and others he consulted carriage-and-earth maps, deploying where troops should enter from.
translations.idiomatic: Day and night he pored over maps with Zuo Wu and others, marking the routes by which his army would march.

sentences.zh: 王曰：「上無太子，宮車即晏駕，大臣必征膠東王，不即常山王，諸侯並爭，吾可以無備乎！

translations.literal: The king said, "The Supreme One has no heir apparent; when the palace carriage then goes on the late drive, the great ministers are sure to summon the King of Jiaodong, if not the King of Changshan; the feudal lords together will contend—how can I be without preparation!
translations.idiomatic: He declared, "The emperor has no heir. When the imperial carriage makes its final journey, the ministers will surely summon the king of Jiaodong or failing him the king of Changshan. The princes will tear one another apart—can I afford to be unprepared?

sentences.zh: 且吾高帝孫，親行仁義，陛下遇我厚，吾能忍之；

translations.literal: Moreover I am the Supreme Emperor's grandson, in person practice benevolence and right; Your Majesty's treatment of me is thick—can I endure it;
translations.idiomatic: I am a grandson of the Gaozu Emperor and have walked the path of benevolence. The Son of Heaven has treated me generously—so far I have borne it;

sentences.zh: 萬世之後，吾寧能北面事豎子乎！」

translations.literal: after ten thousand generations, how could I face north and serve a stripling!"
translations.idiomatic: but when he is gone, am I to bow northward and serve some whelp of his line?

sentences.zh: 王有孽子不害，最長，王不愛，後、太子皆不以為子兄數。

translations.literal: The king had a bastard son Buhei, eldest in age; the king did not love him; the queen and heir apparent both did not treat him in the reckoning of son and elder brother.
translations.idiomatic: The king had an illegitimate eldest son named Liu Buhei, whom he did not favor; neither the queen nor the crown prince acknowledged him as brother or son in the proper order.

sentences.zh: 不害子建，材高有氣，常怨望太子不省其父。

translations.literal: Buhei's son Jian, talented and high-spirited, constantly resented the heir apparent for not visiting his father.
translations.idiomatic: Buhei's son Jian was proud and able; he bitterly resented the crown prince for slighting his father.

sentences.zh: 時，諸侯皆得分子弟為侯，淮南王有兩子，一子為太子，而建父不得為侯。

translations.literal: At the time, all feudal lords could divide sons and younger brothers as marquises; the King of Huainan had two sons, one son was heir apparent, yet Jian's father could not become marquis.
translations.idiomatic: Princes were then allowed to enfeoff younger sons as marquises, but though the king of Huainan had two grown sons—one the crown prince—Jian's father had received no such title.

sentences.zh: 陰結交，欲害太子，以其父代之。

translations.literal: Secretly he formed alliances, wishing to harm the heir apparent, thereby his father replacing him.
translations.idiomatic: Jian formed a secret faction, hoping to destroy the crown prince and put his own father in his place.

sentences.zh: 太子知之，數捕系笞建。

translations.literal: The heir apparent knew it, repeatedly seized, bound, and flogged Jian.
translations.idiomatic: When the crown prince learned of it, he had Jian seized time and again and beaten with the bamboo.

sentences.zh: 建具知太子之欲謀殺漢中尉，即使所善壽春嚴正上書天子曰：「毒藥苦口利病，忠言逆耳利行。

translations.literal: Jian fully knew the heir apparent's wish to plot killing the Han commandant; immediately had one he was close to, Yan Zheng of Shouchun, submit a memorial to the Son of Heaven, saying, "Poison medicine is bitter in the mouth but benefits illness; loyal words offend the ear but benefit conduct."
translations.idiomatic: Jian knew the crown prince had plotted to murder the Han commandant. He had his friend Yan Zheng of Shouchun memorialize the throne: "Bitter medicine heals sickness; unwelcome counsel saves the state."

sentences.zh: 今淮南王孫建材能高，淮南王后荼、荼子遷常疾害建。

translations.literal: Now the King of Huainan's grandson Jian's talent and ability are high; the King of Huainan's queen Tu and Tu's son Qian constantly hate and harm Jian.
translations.idiomatic: The king of Huainan's grandson Jian is a man of outstanding ability, yet Queen Tu and her son Qian persecute him relentlessly.

sentences.zh: 建父不害無罪，擅數系，欲殺之。

translations.literal: Jian's father Buhei was without guilt; yet he was repeatedly seized on authority, they wished to kill him.
translations.idiomatic: Jian's father Buhei is innocent, yet they have jailed him again and again and mean to put him to death.

sentences.zh: 今建在，可征問，具知淮南王陰事。」

translations.literal: Now Jian is present; he can be summoned and questioned—he fully knows the King of Huainan's secret affairs."
translations.idiomatic: Jian is still at hand and can be examined under oath—he knows every secret of the king of Huainan's plotting.

sentences.zh: 書既聞，上以其事下廷尉、河南治。

translations.literal: When the memorial had been heard, the Supreme One sent the affair down to the commandant of justice and Henan for prosecution.
translations.idiomatic: When the memorial reached him, the emperor referred the matter to the commandant of justice and the Henan authorities.

sentences.zh: 是歲元朔六年也。

translations.literal: This year was the sixth year of Yuanshuo.
translations.idiomatic: This was the sixth year of Yuanshuo (123 BCE).

sentences.zh: 故辟陽侯孫審卿善丞相公孫弘，怨淮南厲王殺其大父，陰求淮南事而□之於弘。

translations.literal: The former Marquis of Piyang's grandson Shen Qing was on good terms with Chancellor Gongsun Hong, resented the Prince Li of Huainan for killing his great-grandfather, secretly sought Huainan affairs and [missing graph] them to Hong.
translations.idiomatic: Shen Qing, grandson of the Marquis of Piyang, was friendly with Chancellor Gongsun Hong. He nursed a grudge because Prince Li of Huainan had murdered his grandfather, and he quietly gathered evidence of Liu An's plotting to pass to Hong.

sentences.zh: 弘乃疑淮南有畔逆計，深探其獄。

translations.literal: Hong thereupon suspected Huainan had a treasonous plot, deeply probed the case.
translations.idiomatic: Hong began to suspect a rebellion in Huainan and pushed the investigation to the bottom.

sentences.zh: 河南治建，辭引太子及黨與。

translations.literal: Henan prosecuted Jian; his testimony implicated the heir apparent and accomplices.
translations.idiomatic: Under interrogation in Henan, Jian's confession implicated the crown prince and his confederates.

sentences.zh: 初，王數以舉兵謀問伍被，被常諫之，以吳、楚七國為效。

translations.literal: At first, the king repeatedly asked Wu Bei about plans to raise troops; Bei constantly remonstrated, taking Wu and Chu seven states as a lesson.
translations.idiomatic: At first Liu An often consulted Wu Bei about rebellion, but Wu Bei always dissuaded him, citing the fate of the seven kingdoms of Wu and Chu.

sentences.zh: 王引陳勝、吳廣，被復言形勢不同，必敗亡。

translations.literal: The king cited Chen Sheng and Wu Guang; Bei again said the situation was different, sure to be defeated and destroyed.
translations.idiomatic: The king countered with Chen Sheng and Wu Guang; Wu Bei replied that the times were not the same and such a rising would end in ruin.

sentences.zh: 及建見治，王恐國陰事洩，欲發，復問被，被為言發兵權變。

translations.literal: When Jian came under prosecution, the king feared the state's secret affairs would leak, wished to rise, again asked Bei; Bei for him spoke of raising troops with stratagems and shifts.
translations.idiomatic: When Jian fell under investigation, Liu An feared exposure and again asked Wu Bei about revolt; this time Wu Bei outlined stratagems for raising troops.

sentences.zh: 語在《被傳》。

translations.literal: The words are in the Biography of Bei.
translations.idiomatic: The details are recorded in Wu Bei's biography.

sentences.zh: 於是王銳欲發，乃令官奴入宮中，作皇帝璽，丞相、御史大夫、將軍、吏中二千石、都官令、丞印，及旁近郡太守、都尉印，漢使節法冠。

translations.literal: Thereupon the king keenly wished to rise; he ordered palace slaves to enter the palace, forge the Son of Heaven's seal, the chancellor's, imperial counselor's, generals', mid-two-thousand-dan officials', capital bureau director and assistant seals, and the seals of neighboring commanderies' governors and commandants, Han envoy credentials and law caps.
translations.idiomatic: Burning to act, he sent slaves into the palace to forge the imperial seal, seals for chancellor, imperial counselor, generals, mid-ranking officials, capital bureau directors and assistants, seals of neighboring governors and commandants, and Han credentials and court caps.

sentences.zh: 欲如伍被計，使人為得罪而西，事大將軍、丞相；

translations.literal: Wishing to follow Wu Bei's plan, he sent men to feign guilt and go west, serving the grand general and chancellor;
translations.idiomatic: Following Wu Bei's scheme, he planned to send agents westward under pretense of disgrace to attach themselves to the grand general and the chancellor;

sentences.zh: 一日發兵，即刺大將軍衛青，而說丞相弘下之，如發蒙耳。

translations.literal: the day they raise troops, immediately stab Grand General Wei Qing, and persuade Chancellor Hong to submit—like lifting a blind from the eyes.
translations.idiomatic: On the day of the uprising they would assassinate Grand General Wei Qing and win Chancellor Hong to their side—as easy as lifting a blindfold.

sentences.zh: 欲發國中兵，恐相、二千石不聽，王乃與伍被謀，為失火宮中，相、二千石救火，因殺之。

translations.literal: Wishing to raise troops in the state, he feared the chancellor and two-thousand-dan officials would not obey; the king thereupon plotted with Wu Bei to feign a fire in the palace; when the chancellor and two-thousand-dan officials came to fight the fire, then kill them.
translations.idiomatic: He wished to mobilize the kingdom but feared his chancellor and senior officials would refuse. With Wu Bei he plotted to set a fire in the palace and cut down the chancellor and two-thousand-dan men when they rushed in to fight it.

sentences.zh: 又欲令人衣求盜衣，持羽檄從南方來，呼言曰「南越兵入」，欲因以發兵。

translations.literal: He also wished to order men to wear constable-seeking robes, hold feathered urgent summons coming from the south, crying out saying "Nanyue troops have entered," wishing thereby to raise troops.
translations.idiomatic: He also planned to dress men as constables bearing urgent dispatches from the south, shouting that Nanyue had invaded, as a pretext to call out the army.

sentences.zh: 乃使人之廬江、會稽為求盜，未決。

translations.literal: Thereupon he sent men to Lujiang and Kuaiji to act as constable-seekers; it was not settled.
translations.idiomatic: He had already sent agents to Lujiang and Kuaiji to play the part of constables when the plan was still undecided.

sentences.zh: 廷尉以建辭連太子遷聞，上遣廷尉監與淮南中尉逮捕太子。

translations.literal: The commandant of justice, because Jian's testimony connected the heir apparent Qian, reported it; the Supreme One dispatched the commandant's inspector together with the Huainan commandant to arrest the heir apparent.
translations.idiomatic: When the commandant of justice reported that Jian's confession implicated Crown Prince Qian, the emperor sent his inspector and the Huainan commandant to arrest the prince.

sentences.zh: 至，淮南王聞，與太子謀召相、二千石，欲殺而發兵。

translations.literal: When they arrived, the King of Huainan heard, plotted with the heir apparent to summon the chancellor and two-thousand-dan officials, wishing to kill them and raise troops.
translations.idiomatic: When word reached Liu An, he and the crown prince plotted to summon the chancellor and senior officials to court and murder them before raising the standard of revolt.

sentences.zh: 召相，相至；

translations.literal: They summoned the chancellor; the chancellor came;
translations.idiomatic: The chancellor answered the summons and came;

sentences.zh: 內史以出為解。

translations.literal: the metropolitan governor pleaded being away as excuse.
translations.idiomatic: the metropolitan governor excused himself on the ground that he was out on business.

sentences.zh: 中尉曰：「臣受詔使，不得見王。」

translations.literal: The commandant said, "Your servant received an edict mission; cannot have audience with the king."
translations.idiomatic: The commandant replied, "I am under imperial orders and may not enter audience with Your Highness."

sentences.zh: 王念獨殺相而內史、中尉不來，無益也，即罷相。

translations.literal: The king thought that to kill only the chancellor while the metropolitan governor and commandant did not come would be without benefit; he immediately dismissed the chancellor.
translations.idiomatic: Liu An saw that killing the chancellor alone, while the metropolitan governor and commandant stayed away, would gain nothing, and he let the chancellor go.

sentences.zh: 計猶與未決。

translations.literal: The plan hesitated and was not decided.
translations.idiomatic: He still wavered and could not decide.

sentences.zh: 太子念所坐者謀殺漢中尉，所與謀殺者已死，以為口絕，及謂王曰：「群臣可用者皆前系，今無足與舉事者。

translations.literal: The heir apparent thought that what he was charged with was plotting to kill the Han commandant; those with whom he plotted to kill were already dead; he considered the mouths cut off; and said to the king, "Among the host of ministers those who could be used were all earlier bound; now there are none sufficient to join in raising the affair."
translations.idiomatic: The crown prince reflected that his only proven crime was plotting to murder the Han commandant and that his accomplices in that plot were already dead, so witnesses were silenced. He told his father, "Every minister we might have relied on is already in custody; there is no one left to strike with."

sentences.zh: 王以非時發，恐無功，臣願會逮。」

translations.literal: If the king raises troops at an untimely moment, I fear there will be no success; your servant wishes to submit to arrest."
translations.idiomatic: If you rise out of season, Father, I fear we shall accomplish nothing. Let me go and answer the summons myself."

sentences.zh: 王亦愈欲休，即許太子。

translations.literal: The king also increasingly wished to rest; immediately granted the heir apparent.
translations.idiomatic: The king himself was losing heart and consented.

sentences.zh: 太子自刑，不殊。

translations.literal: The heir apparent wounded himself; did not sever completely.
translations.idiomatic: The crown prince attempted suicide but failed to finish the deed.

sentences.zh: 伍被自詣吏，具告與淮南王謀反。

translations.literal: Wu Bei himself went to the clerks and fully reported plotting rebellion with the King of Huainan.
translations.idiomatic: Wu Bei surrendered to the authorities and confessed the whole conspiracy with Liu An.

sentences.zh: 吏因捕太子、王后，圍王宮，盡捕王賓客在國中者，索得反具以聞。

translations.literal: The clerks thereupon seized the heir apparent and queen, surrounded the royal palace, seized to the last the king's guests in the state, searched and obtained rebellion tools and reported.
translations.idiomatic: Officers then arrested the crown prince and queen, sealed the palace, rounded up every retainer of the king still in the kingdom, and turned up the forged seals and arms of rebellion.

sentences.zh: 上下公卿治，所連引與淮南王謀反列侯、二千石、豪桀數千人，皆以罪輕重受誅。

translations.literal: It was sent down to dukes and ministers for trial; those implicated and drawn in with the King of Huainan in plotting rebellion—full marquises, two-thousand-dan men, and stalwart heroes numbering several thousand—all according to guilt light or heavy received execution.
