translations.idiomatic: The case went to the high ministers. Several thousand marquises, two-thousand-dan officials, and local strongmen implicated in the plot were executed according to the gravity of their offenses.

sentences.zh: 衡山王賜，淮南王弟，當坐收。

translations.literal: The King of Hengshan Ci, younger brother of the King of Huainan, ought by guilt to be seized.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Ci, king of Hengshan and the king of Huainan's brother, was liable to arrest as an accessory.

sentences.zh: 有司請逮捕衡山王，上曰：「諸侯各以其國為本，不當相坐。

translations.literal: The responsible officials requested to arrest the King of Hengshan; the Supreme One said, "Each feudal lord takes his state as root; ought not implicate one another by guilt."
translations.idiomatic: When the ministry asked to arrest the king of Hengshan, the emperor replied, "Each prince stands or falls by his own domain; they must not be punished for one another's crimes."

sentences.zh: 與諸侯王列侯議。」

translations.literal: Deliberate with kings of feudal lords and full marquises."
translations.idiomatic: Lay the matter before the kings and marquises."

sentences.zh: 趙王彭祖、列侯讓等四十三人皆曰：「淮南王安大逆無道，謀反明白，當伏誅。」

translations.literal: The King of Zhao Pengzu, Marquis Rang and others forty-three persons all said, "The King of Huainan An is greatly rebellious and without the Way; plotting rebellion is clear and bright—ought to suffer execution."
translations.idiomatic: Forty-three princes and marquises, including Liu Pengzu of Zhao and Marquis Rang, declared in one voice that Liu An had committed the supreme crime, that his treason was manifest, and that he must die.

sentences.zh: 膠西王端議曰：「安廢法度，行邪辟，有詐偽心，以亂天下，營惑百姓，背畔宗廟，妄作妖言。

translations.literal: The King of Jiaoxi Duan deliberated, saying, "An casts aside laws and measures, practices depravity, has a deceitful heart, thereby to disorder all under Heaven, deludes the hundred surnames, turns his back on and rebels against the ancestral temple, recklessly makes prodigious words."
translations.idiomatic: Liu Duan of Jiaoxi argued: "Liu An has trampled the law, followed crooked ways, and harbored deceit to throw the realm into chaos. He has misled the people, betrayed his ancestors, and spread seditious talk."

sentences.zh: 《春秋》曰『臣毋將，將而誅』。

translations.literal: The Spring and Autumn says, 'A minister must not lead troops; if he leads troops, then execute him.'
translations.idiomatic: The Spring and Autumn Annals say, 'No minister may take command of troops against his lord; if he does, he dies.'

sentences.zh: 安罪重於將，謀反形已定。

translations.literal: An's crime is heavier than leading troops; plotting rebellion's form is already fixed.
translations.idiomatic: Liu An's offense is graver than mere mutiny; the shape of his rebellion is already plain.

sentences.zh: 臣端所見其書印圖及它逆亡道事驗明白，當伏法。

translations.literal: What your servant Duan has seen—his writings, seals, maps, and other contrary and lawless matters—evidence is clear and bright; he ought to submit to the law.
translations.idiomatic: The documents, seals, and maps I have seen, together with his other lawless acts, prove the case beyond doubt. He must answer to the law.

sentences.zh: 論國吏二百石以上及比者，宗室近幸臣不在法中者，不能相教，皆當免，削爵為士伍，毋得官為吏。

translations.literal: As for state clerks of two hundred dan and above and those comparable, imperial kin and favored ministers not within the law who could not mutually instruct—all ought to be dismissed, cut rank to common soldier, not permitted to hold office as clerks.
translations.idiomatic: Every official of the kingdom from two hundred dan upward, together with imperial kinsmen and favored attendants who, though not themselves indictable under the statutes, failed to restrain him, should be dismissed, stripped of rank to commoner status, and barred forever from office.

sentences.zh: 其非吏，它贖死金二斤八兩，以章安之罪，使天下明知臣子之道，毋敢復有邪僻背畔之意。」

translations.literal: Those who are not clerks, separately may ransom death with two jin eight liang of gold, thereby to display An's guilt, causing all under Heaven clearly to know the way of minister and son, none daring again to have depraved rebellious intent.
translations.idiomatic: Those who were not officials may commute the death penalty with two jin eight liang of gold. Thus Liu An's guilt will be plain to the empire and every subject will know the duty owed a sovereign—none will dare harbor treason again.

sentences.zh: 丞相弘、廷尉湯等以聞，上使宗正以符節治王。

translations.literal: Chancellor Hong, Commandant of Justice Tang and others reported it; the Supreme One ordered the director of the imperial clan with credentials and baton to try the king.
translations.idiomatic: When Chancellor Gongsun Hong and Commandant Zhang Tang reported the verdict, the emperor sent the director of the imperial clan with full credentials to pass sentence on the king.

sentences.zh: 未至，安自刑殺。

translations.literal: Before he arrived, An killed himself by violence.
translations.idiomatic: Before the messenger reached him, Liu An took his own life.

sentences.zh: 後、太子諸所與謀皆收夷。

translations.literal: The queen, the heir apparent, and all who plotted with them were seized and exterminated.
translations.idiomatic: The queen, the crown prince, and every co-conspirator were arrested and executed to the last degree.

sentences.zh: 國除為九江郡。

translations.literal: The state was abolished and made Jiujiang commandery.
translations.idiomatic: The kingdom was abolished and reorganized as Jiujiang commandery.

sentences.zh: 衡山王賜後乘舒生子三人，長男爽為太子，次女無采，少男孝。

translations.literal: The King of Hengshan Ci's queen Chengshu bore three children: the eldest son Shuang became heir apparent, the next daughter Wucai, the youngest son Xiao.
translations.idiomatic: King Liu Ci of Hengshan and his queen Chengshu had three children: the eldest son Shuang, who was heir apparent; a daughter named Wucai; and a younger son named Xiao.

sentences.zh: 姬徐來生子男女四人，美人厥姬生子二人。

translations.literal: Concubine Xulai bore four children male and female; the beauty Jueji bore two sons.
translations.idiomatic: A concubine named Xulai bore four children, and the beauty Jueji bore two sons.

sentences.zh: 淮南、衡山相責望禮節，間不相能。

translations.literal: Huainan and Hengshan mutually blamed and expected in ritual propriety; gradually they did not get along.
translations.idiomatic: The courts of Huainan and Hengshan quarreled over etiquette and protocol until the two houses were barely on speaking terms.

sentences.zh: 衡山王聞淮南王作為畔逆具，亦心結賓客以應之，恐為所並。

translations.literal: When the King of Hengshan heard that the King of Huainan was making tools for rebellion, he also in his heart gathered guests to respond to him, fearing to be annexed by him.
translations.idiomatic: When Liu Ci learned that Liu An was forging the instruments of revolt, he began recruiting retainers of his own, fearing annexation if Huainan struck first.

sentences.zh: 元光六年入朝，謁者衛慶有方術，欲上書事天子，王怒，故劾慶死罪，強榜服之。

translations.literal: In the sixth year of Yuanguang he entered court audience; the usher Wei Qing had formulas and techniques, wished to submit a memorial serving the Son of Heaven; the king was angry, therefore impeached Qing for capital crime, forced flogging until he confessed.
translations.idiomatic: In 129 BCE, while attending court, an usher named Wei Qing who knew occult arts sought to memorialize the emperor directly. The king was furious, framed Wei Qing for a capital offense, and had him tortured until he confessed.

sentences.zh: 內史以為非是，卻其獄。

translations.literal: The metropolitan governor considered it not right and rejected the case.
translations.idiomatic: The metropolitan governor saw the injustice and threw out the prosecution.

sentences.zh: 王使人上書告內史，內史治，言王不直。

translations.literal: The king sent men to submit a memorial accusing the metropolitan governor; the metropolitan governor prosecuted, saying the king was not upright.
translations.idiomatic: The king retaliated with a memorial against the metropolitan governor, who in turn proved the king in the wrong.

sentences.zh: 又數侵奪人田，壞人塚以為田。

translations.literal: Moreover repeatedly he encroached and seized people's fields, destroyed people's tombs to make fields.
translations.idiomatic: He repeatedly seized private land and plowed up graves to add to his estates.

sentences.zh: 有司請逮治衡山王，上不許，為置吏二百石以上。

translations.literal: The responsible officials requested to seize and try the King of Hengshan; the Supreme One did not permit, and for him appointed clerks of two hundred dan and above.
translations.idiomatic: When the ministry asked permission to arrest him, the emperor refused but posted officials of two hundred dan rank or higher throughout his kingdom.

sentences.zh: 衡山王以此恚，與奚慈、張廣昌謀，求能為兵法候星氣者，日夜縱臾王謀反事。

translations.literal: The King of Hengshan for this resented, plotted with Xi Ci and Zhang Guangchang, sought those who could practice military arts and observe stellar vapors, day and night urged and pressed the king on matters of plotting rebellion.
translations.idiomatic: Smarting under the humiliation, Liu Ci conspired with Xi Ci and Zhang Guangchang, sought out experts in strategy and astrology, and had them urge him toward rebellion day and night.

sentences.zh: 後乘舒死，立徐來為後，厥姬俱幸。

translations.literal: After Chengshu died, he established Xulai as queen; Jueji both received favor.
translations.idiomatic: After Queen Chengshu died, Liu Ci made Xulai his new queen while continuing to favor Jueji as well.

sentences.zh: 兩人相妒。

translations.literal: The two persons mutually were jealous.
translations.idiomatic: The two women hated each other.

sentences.zh: 厥姬乃惡徐來於太子，曰：「徐來使婢蠱殺太子母。」

translations.literal: Jueji thereupon slandered Xulai to the heir apparent, saying, "Xulai ordered maids to witchcraft-kill the heir apparent's mother."
translations.idiomatic: Jueji poisoned the crown prince's mind against the new queen, claiming she had used witchcraft and a slave girl to murder his mother.

sentences.zh: 太子心怨徐來。

translations.literal: The heir apparent in his heart resented Xulai.
translations.idiomatic: The crown prince nursed a deep grudge against Xulai.

sentences.zh: 徐來兄至衡山，太子與飲，以刃刑傷之。

translations.literal: Xulai's elder brother arrived at Hengshan; the heir apparent drank with him and with a blade wounded him by cutting.
translations.idiomatic: When Xulai's brother visited Hengshan, the crown prince feasted him and then slashed him with a knife.

sentences.zh: 後以此怨太子，數惡之於王。

translations.literal: The queen for this resented the heir apparent, repeatedly slandered him to the king.
translations.idiomatic: The queen never forgave the prince and slandered him to the king at every turn.

sentences.zh: 女弟無采嫁，棄歸，與客奸。

translations.literal: The younger sister Wucai married, was cast off and returned, and fornicated with a guest.
translations.idiomatic: His sister Wucai had married, been divorced, and returned home, where she took lovers among the retainers.

sentences.zh: 太子數以數讓之，無采怒，不與太子通。

translations.literal: The heir apparent repeatedly with reproaches blamed her; Wucai was angry and did not communicate with the heir apparent.
translations.idiomatic: The crown prince scolded her repeatedly until she broke with him in fury and refused to speak to him.

sentences.zh: 後聞之，即善遇無采及孝。

translations.literal: When the queen heard it, immediately she treated well Wucai and Xiao.
translations.idiomatic: When the queen heard of the rift, she showered Wucai and Xiao with kindness.

sentences.zh: 孝少失母，附後，後以計愛之，與共毀太子，王以故數系笞太子。

translations.literal: Xiao had young lost his mother, attached to the queen; the queen by stratagem loved him, together with him slandered the heir apparent; the king for this reason repeatedly bound and flogged the heir apparent.
translations.idiomatic: Xiao had lost his mother in childhood and clung to the queen, who feigned affection and joined him in blackening the crown prince until the king repeatedly had the prince bound and beaten.

sentences.zh: 元朔四年中，人有賊傷後假母者，王疑太子使人傷之，笞太子。

translations.literal: In the midst of the fourth year of Yuanshuo, someone villainously wounded the queen's foster mother; the king suspected the heir apparent sent men to wound her and flogged the heir apparent.
translations.idiomatic: In 125 BCE someone attacked the queen's nurse. The king blamed the crown prince and had him flogged.

sentences.zh: 後王病，太子時稱病不侍。

translations.literal: Afterward when the king was ill, the heir apparent sometimes claimed illness and did not attend.
translations.idiomatic: Later, when the king fell ill, the crown prince often pleaded sickness and refused to attend him.

sentences.zh: 孝、無采惡太子：「實不病，自言，有喜色。」

translations.literal: Xiao and Wucai slandered the heir apparent: "In truth he is not ill; he speaks of himself with joyful expression."
translations.idiomatic: Xiao and Wucai told the king, "He is not sick at all—he boasts of it and looks pleased with himself."

sentences.zh: 王於是大怒，欲廢太子而立弟孝。

translations.literal: The king thereupon was greatly angry, wished to depose the heir apparent and establish the younger brother Xiao.
translations.idiomatic: The king flew into a rage and resolved to depose Shuang in favor of his younger brother Xiao.

sentences.zh: 後知王決廢太子，又欲並廢孝。

translations.literal: When the queen knew the king had decided to depose the heir apparent, she also wished together to depose Xiao.
translations.idiomatic: Learning that the king meant to remove the crown prince, the queen schemed to bring Xiao down as well.

sentences.zh: 後有侍者善舞，王幸之，後欲令與孝亂以污之，欲並廢二子而以己子廣代之。

translations.literal: The queen had a attendant skilled in dance whom the king favored; the queen wished to make her commit disorder with Xiao to defile him, wishing together to depose both sons and replace them with her own son Guang.
translations.idiomatic: There was a dancing girl the king favored. The queen tried to force her into Xiao's bed to ruin his name, hoping to disinherit both princes and install her own son Guang as heir.

sentences.zh: 太子知之，念後數惡己無已時，欲與亂以止其口。

translations.literal: The heir apparent knew it, thought that the queen repeatedly slandered him without end; he wished to violate her thereby to stop her mouth.
translations.idiomatic: The crown prince saw through the plot but reasoned that the queen would never stop slandering him unless he could silence her with a scandal of his own.

sentences.zh: 後飲太子，太子前為壽，因據後股求與臥。

translations.literal: The queen feasted the heir apparent; the heir apparent came forward to offer longevity toast, then grasped the queen's thigh seeking to lie with her.
translations.idiomatic: At a banquet she gave for him, he came forward with a toast, seized her by the thigh, and demanded that she sleep with him.

sentences.zh: 後怒，以告王。

translations.literal: The queen was angry and reported it to the king.
translations.idiomatic: She burst out in rage and ran to the king with the story.

sentences.zh: 王乃召，欲縛笞之。

translations.literal: The king thereupon summoned him, wishing to bind and flog him.
translations.idiomatic: The king called for the prince to be bound and flogged.

sentences.zh: 太子知王常欲廢己而立孝，乃謂王曰：「孝與王御者奸，無采與奴奸，王強食，請上書。」

translations.literal: The heir apparent knew the king always wished to depose him and establish Xiao; thereupon said to the king, "Xiao fornicates with the king's charioteer; Wucai fornicates with a slave—Your Majesty force yourself to eat; I request to submit a memorial."
translations.idiomatic: Knowing his father meant to replace him with Xiao, the crown prince shouted back, "Xiao lies with one of your coachmen, and Wucai with a house slave. Swallow your meal if you can, Father—I am going to memorialize the throne!"

sentences.zh: 即背王去。

translations.literal: Immediately he turned his back on the king and departed.
translations.idiomatic: He spun on his heel and fled.

sentences.zh: 王使人止之，莫能禁，王乃自追捕太子。

translations.literal: The king sent men to stop him; none could restrain him; the king thereupon himself pursued and seized the heir apparent.
translations.idiomatic: Servants tried to stop him but could not; the king chased him down in person.

sentences.zh: 太子妄惡言，王械系宮中。

translations.literal: The heir apparent spoke reckless evil words; the king fettered and bound him in the palace.
translations.idiomatic: The prince hurled wild accusations until the king had him shackled in the inner palace.

sentences.zh: 孝日益以親幸。

translations.literal: Xiao daily became more intimate and favored.
translations.idiomatic: Day by day Xiao grew closer to the king's favor.

sentences.zh: 王奇孝材能，乃佩之王印，號曰將軍，令居外家，多給金錢；

translations.literal: The king marveled at Xiao's talent and ability, thereupon hung on him the king's seal, titled him General, ordered him to dwell at his maternal relatives' house, much supplying gold and cash;
translations.idiomatic: Impressed by Xiao's abilities, the king hung his own seal on the boy's belt, styled him "general," quartered him with his mother's kin, and poured money into his hands;

sentences.zh: 招致賓客。

translations.literal: and summoned guests.
translations.idiomatic: and let him recruit retainers.

sentences.zh: 賓客來者，微知淮南、衡山有逆計，皆將養勸之。

translations.literal: Guests who came, slightly knowing Huainan and Hengshan had treasonous plans, all supported and urged him.
translations.idiomatic: Those who drifted in, catching wind of plots in Huainan and Hengshan, fed and flattered him toward rebellion.

sentences.zh: 王乃使孝客江都人枚赫、陳喜作輣車鍛矢，刻天子璽，將、相、軍吏印。

translations.literal: The king thereupon ordered Xiao's guests, the Jiangdu men Mei He and Chen Xi, to make covered assault carts and forge arrows, carve the Son of Heaven's seal, and seals of generals, chancellors, and military clerks.
translations.idiomatic: He set Xiao's retainers Mei He and Chen Xi of Jiangdu to building assault wagons and forging arrowheads, carving a counterfeit imperial seal and seals for generals, chancellors, and army officers.

sentences.zh: 王日夜求壯士如周丘等，數稱引吳、楚反時計畫約束。

translations.literal: The king day and night sought stalwart men such as Zhou Qiu, repeatedly citing and quoting the plans and agreements from the time of Wu and Chu's rebellion.
translations.idiomatic: Night and day he sought bravoes like Zhou Qiu and endlessly reviewed the strategy of the old Wu-Chu revolt.

sentences.zh: 衡山王非敢效淮南王求即天子位，畏淮南起並其國，以為淮南已西，發兵定江淮間而有之，望如是。

translations.literal: The King of Hengshan did not dare imitate the King of Huainan in seeking immediately the Son of Heaven's position; he feared if Huainan rose he would annex his state; he thought that if Huainan had already gone west, raising troops he would pacify the region between the Yangzi and Huai and possess it—his hope was thus.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Ci never dreamed of seizing the throne like Liu An; he feared that if Huainan rose first his kingdom would be swallowed. His hope was that once Huainan marched west, he could raise troops, secure the land between the Yangzi and the Huai, and hold it for himself.

sentences.zh: 元朔五年秋，當朝，六年，過淮南。

translations.literal: In the autumn of the fifth year of Yuanshuo, he ought to have attended court; in the sixth year, he passed through Huainan.
translations.idiomatic: In the autumn of 124 BCE he was due at court; the following year he traveled through Huainan on his way.

sentences.zh: 淮南王乃昆弟語，除前隙，約束反具。

translations.literal: The King of Huainan then spoke fraternal words, removed former rifts, covenanted and agreed on tools for rebellion.
translations.idiomatic: The king of Huainan received him as a brother, buried old quarrels, and the two princes pledged each other to the conspiracy.

sentences.zh: 衡山王即上書謝病，上賜不朝。

translations.literal: The King of Hengshan immediately submitted a memorial apologizing for illness; the Supreme One granted not to attend court.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Ci memorialized illness, and the emperor excused him from court.

sentences.zh: 乃使人上書請廢太子爽，立孝為太子。

translations.literal: Thereupon he sent men to submit a memorial requesting to depose the heir apparent Shuang and establish Xiao as heir apparent.
translations.idiomatic: He then had a memorial sent asking to depose Crown Prince Shuang and name Xiao heir instead.

sentences.zh: 爽聞，即使所善白嬴之長安上書，言衡山王與子謀逆，言孝作兵車鍛矢，與王御者奸。

translations.literal: When Shuang heard, immediately he had one he favored, Bai Ying, go to Chang'an to submit a memorial, saying the King of Hengshan plotted rebellion with his son, saying Xiao made military carts and forged arrows, and fornicated with the king's charioteer.
translations.idiomatic: Shuang countered by sending his confidant Bai Ying to the capital with a memorial accusing his father and brother of treason—charging Xiao with building war wagons and forging arms and with adultery with one of the king's grooms.

sentences.zh: 至長安未及上書，即吏捕贏，以淮南事系。

translations.literal: When he reached Chang'an before submitting the memorial, immediately clerks arrested Ying, on account of the Huainan affair bound him.
translations.idiomatic: Bai Ying was seized in Chang'an before he could file the document, held on charges stemming from the Huainan case.

sentences.zh: 王聞之，恐其言國陰事，即上書告太子，以為不道。

translations.literal: When the king heard it, fearing he would speak the state's secret affairs, immediately submitted a memorial accusing the heir apparent, considering him without the Way.
translations.idiomatic: Fearing Bai Ying would expose the kingdom's secrets, the king raced a counter-memorial to the throne denouncing the crown prince for depravity.

sentences.zh: 事下沛郡治。

translations.literal: The affair was sent down to Pei commandery for trial.
translations.idiomatic: The case was referred to Pei commandery for investigation.

sentences.zh: 濟北貞王勃者，景帝四年徙。

translations.literal: The Prince Zhen of Jibei Bo—in the fourth year of Emperor Jing was transferred.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Bo, posthumously known as the Prince Zhen of Jibei, was transferred to that kingdom in the fourth year of Emperor Jing's reign (153 BCE).

sentences.zh: 徙二年，因前王衡山，凡十四年薨。

translations.literal: Two years after transfer, counting his former kingship of Hengshan, in all fourteen years he died.
translations.idiomatic: Two years after the move, reckoning his prior reign as king of Hengshan, he had held a throne fourteen years in all when he died.

sentences.zh: 子式王胡嗣，五十四年薨。

translations.literal: The son, Prince Shi Hu, succeeded; in fifty-four years he died.
translations.idiomatic: His son Liu Hu, posthumously Prince Shi of Jibei, inherited the title and ruled fifty-four years.

sentences.zh: 子寬嗣。

translations.literal: The son Kuan succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: His son Liu Kuan succeeded him.

sentences.zh: 十二年，寬坐與父式王后光、姬孝兒奸，悖人倫，又祠祭祝詛上，有司請誅。

translations.literal: In the twelfth year, Kuan sat guilty of fornicating with his father Prince Shi's queen Guang and concubine Xiao'er, violating human relations; moreover in sacrifice he prayed and cursed the Supreme One; the responsible officials requested execution.
translations.idiomatic: In the twelfth year of his reign Liu Kuan was convicted of incest with his late father's queen Guang and a concubine named Xiao'er, a violation of the most basic human ethics, and of cursing the emperor in his ancestral sacrifices. The ministry demanded his death.

sentences.zh: 上遣大鴻臚利召王，王以刃自剄死。

translations.literal: The Supreme One sent the grand herald Li to summon the king; the king with a blade cut his own throat and died.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor sent Grand Herald Li to summon him to the capital; Liu Kuan cut his own throat before he could be taken.

sentences.zh: 國除為北安縣，屬泰山郡。

translations.literal: The state was abolished and made Bei'an county, belonging to Taishan commandery.
translations.idiomatic: The kingdom was abolished and reorganized as Bei'an county under Taishan commandery.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：《詩》云「戎狄是膺，荊舒是懲」，信哉是言也！

translations.literal: The appraisal says: The Odes says, "The Rong and Di are smitten, Jing and Shu are punished"—truly this saying!
translations.idiomatic: The historian's appraisal: The Classic of Poetry says, "Punish the Rong and Di, chastise Jing and Shu"—how true those words ring!

sentences.zh: 淮南、衡山親為骨肉，疆土千里，列在諸侯，不務遵蕃臣職，以丞輔天子，而剸懷邪辟之計，謀為畔逆，仍父子再亡國，各不終其身。

translations.literal: Huainan and Hengshan were kin as bone and flesh, territory a thousand li, arrayed among feudal lords; not striving to observe the duty of vassal ministers, thereby to assist the Son of Heaven, but wholly embracing depraved plans, plotting to be rebellious—twice father and son lost their states, each not completing his body.
translations.idiomatic: Huainan and Hengshan were flesh and blood of the imperial house, each ruling a thousand li as a feudal prince, yet neither strove to keep the duty of a vassal who supports the Son of Heaven. Instead they clutched treason in their hearts—and twice, father and son, they lost their kingdoms, neither living out his natural span.

sentences.zh: 此非獨王也，亦其俗薄，臣下漸靡使然。

translations.literal: This was not only the kings; also their customs were thin, ministers and subordinates gradually worn down caused it.
translations.idiomatic: The fault lay not in the kings alone: the moral climate had thinned and their ministers had dragged them by degrees to ruin.

sentences.zh: 夫荊楚剽輕，好作亂，乃自古記之矣。

translations.literal: Now Jing and Chu are nimble and light, fond of making rebellion—from antiquity it has been recorded thus.
translations.idiomatic: The men of Jing and Chu have ever been a restless, volatile people, prone to rebellion—the histories have said so since ancient times.
